1.title: Chapter 1: The Clocks Strike Thirteen
1.text:
It was a bright cold day in April, and the clocks were striking thirteen. Winston Smith, his chin nuzzled into his breast in an effort to escape the vile wind, slipped quickly through the glass doors of Victory Mansions, though not quickly enough to prevent a swirl of gritty dust from entering along with him.

The hallway smelt of boiled cabbage and old rag mats. At one end of it a coloured poster, too large for indoor display, had been tacked to the wall. It depicted simply an enormous face, more than a metre wide: the face of a man of about forty-five, with a heavy black moustache and ruggedly handsome features. BIG BROTHER IS WATCHING YOU, the caption beneath it ran.

Winston was thirty-nine and had a varicose ulcer above his right ankle. He climbed the stairs slowly — the lift never worked. On each landing, the poster with the enormous face gazed from the wall. It was one of those pictures which are so contrived that the eyes follow you about when you move.

Inside his flat, a telescreen on the wall could not be shut off. It received and transmitted simultaneously. Any sound that Winston made, above the level of a very low whisper, would be picked up by it. There was no way of knowing whether you were being watched at any given moment.

Winston worked at the Ministry of Truth — Minitrue, in Newspeak — where his job was to alter historical records. If the Party said that Oceania had always been at war with Eastasia, then history had to show that this was true. Newspapers, books, photographs — all were corrected. The past was whatever the Party said it was.

He lived in London, chief city of Airstrip One, the third most populous province of Oceania. The Party controlled everything. At the head was Big Brother — though whether Big Brother actually existed as a person, or was merely a symbol, no one knew.

Three slogans were carved into the white face of the Ministry of Truth:

WAR IS PEACE.
FREEDOM IS SLAVERY.
IGNORANCE IS STRENGTH.

That evening, Winston did something dangerous. He sat in the alcove beside the telescreen — the one spot in his flat where the camera could not see — opened a diary he had secretly purchased, and began to write.

"April 4th, 1984. Down with Big Brother."

The thought-crime was done. Whether he wrote it or did not write it made no difference. The Thought Police would get him just the same.

2.title: Chapter 2: The Dark-Haired Girl
2.text:
At the Ministry of Truth, Winston noticed two people. One was O'Brien — a member of the Inner Party, a large, burly man with a thick neck and a coarse, humorous, brutal face. Despite his appearance, he had a certain charm of manner. Winston had a secret belief — or hope — that O'Brien's political orthodoxy was not perfect. Something in his face suggested it.

The other was a dark-haired girl from the Fiction Department. She wore the narrow scarlet sash of the Junior Anti-Sex League wound several times round the waist of her overalls. Winston hated her. She was young and pretty and sexless — exactly the type who were the most bigoted adherents of the Party. He was sure she was an agent of the Thought Police.

During the Two Minutes Hate — the daily ritual where Party members screamed their fury at Emmanuel Goldstein, the Enemy of the People — Winston caught O'Brien's eye. For perhaps half a second their eyes met, and Winston was certain that O'Brien was thinking the same thing as himself.

Days later, the dark-haired girl slipped something into Winston's hand in the corridor. His heart hammered. He waited hours before daring to unfold the scrap of paper.

It said: I LOVE YOU.

Her name was Julia. She was twenty-six, outwardly the model Party member — she volunteered for community hikes, organized demonstrations, served on the committee of the Junior Anti-Sex League. But beneath it all, she despised the Party.

"I'm good at spotting people who don't belong," Julia told him. "As soon as I saw you I knew you were against them."

They began meeting in secret — in the countryside, in the belfry of a ruined church, always moving, always watching for microphones and informers. Julia was practical about rebellion. She didn't care about ideology or the brotherhood of man. She cared about living — about pleasure, about love, about being human in a world designed to crush humanity.

"They can make you say anything — anything — but they can't make you believe it," Julia said. "They can't get inside you."

Winston was not so sure.

3.title: Chapter 3: The Room Above the Shop
3.text:
Winston rented a room above Mr. Charrington's antique shop in the proles' quarter. It was a foolish risk — the room had no telescreen, which made it simultaneously perfect and suspicious. But for the first time in his life, Winston had a space that felt like freedom.

He and Julia met there regularly. They made real coffee (bought on the black market), they made love, they talked. The room with its mahogany bed, its old-fashioned clock, and its steel engraving of St Clement's Church became their private world.

Julia brought luxuries — real sugar, real bread, real tea. She had a talent for the black market. She painted her face and wore scent — acts of rebellion in a society where beauty was suspect.

"Do you realize that the past, starting from yesterday, has been actually abolished?" Winston said to her one evening. "Every record has been destroyed or falsified, every book rewritten, every picture repainted. History has stopped."

Julia was less troubled by the philosophical implications. "Who cares?" she said. "It's always one bloody war after another, and one knows the news is all lies anyway."

Winston tried to explain his deeper terror: the Party's assault on objective truth itself. If the Party said two plus two equals five, then it was five. If the Party said the earth was flat, then it was flat. It was not merely that they forced you to say it — they demanded that you believe it.

"Freedom is the freedom to say that two plus two make four," Winston had written in his diary. "If that is granted, all else follows."

The coral paperweight Winston had bought from Mr. Charrington seemed to symbolize their fragile world — a tiny, beautiful thing, preserved under glass, separate from the vast gray machinery of the Party.

They knew it could not last. Every moment of happiness was borrowed time. But for now, the room above the shop was enough.

4.title: Chapter 4: O'Brien's Invitation
4.text:
O'Brien made his move. At the Ministry, he approached Winston and mentioned a Newspeak dictionary, suggesting Winston visit his flat to pick up advance copies. It was the invitation Winston had been waiting for — or dreading.

Winston and Julia went together to O'Brien's flat in the Inner Party quarter. The luxury was staggering — real wine, a telescreen that could actually be turned off. O'Brien confirmed what Winston had suspected: he was a member of the Brotherhood, the legendary underground resistance led by Emmanuel Goldstein.

O'Brien asked them how far they were willing to go. Would they commit murder? Yes. Sabotage? Yes. Betray their country? Yes. Separate and never see each other again? Yes.

"You are prepared to commit suicide?" O'Brien asked.

"Yes," they both said.

"There is only one thing you must never do," O'Brien warned. "You must never betray the inner feelings in your heart. If they make you confess — confess everything. But do not let them reach your soul."

He gave Winston a copy of Goldstein's book — THE THEORY AND PRACTICE OF OLIGARCHICAL COLLECTIVISM. It explained everything: how the Party maintained power, why the wars were perpetual, how Newspeak was designed to make thoughtcrime literally impossible by eliminating the words for it.

The book confirmed what Winston had intuited. The Party's purpose was not to improve society. The Party's purpose was power. Pure power. Power as an end in itself.

"The Party seeks power entirely for its own sake," the book explained. "Power is not a means; it is an end. The object of persecution is persecution. The object of torture is torture. The object of power is power."

Winston read the book in the room above the shop, with Julia beside him. She fell asleep as he read. He watched her breathing and felt a tenderness that was, he knew, the most dangerous thing in the world.

For the first time, he allowed himself to believe that the Brotherhood was real, that resistance was possible, that the Party could be overthrown. It was the most foolish and the most human thing he had ever done.

5.title: Chapter 5: The Arrest
5.text:
They were lying in bed in the room above the shop when the voice came from behind the picture on the wall.

"You are the dead," said an iron voice.

"You are the dead," echoed Julia.

"You are the dead," the voice repeated. It was a telescreen — hidden behind the picture of St Clement's Church. They had been watched the entire time.

The room filled with black-uniformed men. They smashed the coral paperweight on the hearthstone. The tiny world inside it shattered — the fragment of coral, pink and beautiful, rolled across the floor like a miniature death.

Mr. Charrington entered the room. He was transformed. The old, stooping antique dealer with his gentle manner and scholarly interest in the past — all of it had been a performance. He was a member of the Thought Police. He looked younger, harder, his voice was different.

"It was behind the picture," he said to the officers, as if confirming a technical detail.

They were separated immediately. Winston caught one last glimpse of Julia's face — white, terrified — before a guard punched him in the stomach and he doubled over. They dragged him down the stairs and into a waiting van.

He was taken to the Ministry of Love — Miniluv. The building had no windows. The lights burned day and night. There was no way to tell what time it was, what day it was. The corridors were white and humming.

In his cell, Winston waited. Other prisoners came and went — ordinary people, Party members, proles. Some were beaten. Some were taken away and never returned. A man named Ampleforth, a poet who had let the word "God" remain in a Kipling translation, was brought in and taken away. Parsons, Winston's neighbor — the enthusiastic Party member whose own daughter had denounced him — was there too, blubbering with terror.

"Thoughtcrime," Parsons whispered. "My daughter heard me talking in my sleep. 'Down with Big Brother,' she says I said."

The cell door opened. Guards entered, and behind them — O'Brien.

Winston's heart lurched. "They got you too!" he cried.

"They got me a long time ago," O'Brien said quietly.

He had never been part of the Brotherhood. There was no Brotherhood. The book, the meetings, the wine, the telescreen that could be turned off — all of it was a trap. O'Brien was not a rebel. He was the Party's instrument, and he had been watching Winston for seven years.

6.title: Chapter 6: Room 101
6.text:
The torture was systematic and endless. O'Brien was Winston's interrogator, his teacher, and his tormentor. He inflicted pain with the calm precision of a surgeon — always explaining, always reasonable, always returning to the same questions.

"How many fingers am I holding up, Winston?"

"Four."

O'Brien turned the dial. Pain blazed through Winston's body. "How many fingers?"

"Four! Four! What else can I say? Four!"

"How many fingers, Winston?"

"Five! Five! Five!"

"No, Winston, that is no use. You are lying. You still think there are four. How many fingers, please?"

The goal was not merely to make Winston say five. The goal was to make him see five. To destroy his ability to perceive reality independent of the Party's will. Two plus two must genuinely equal five — not as a lie, but as truth.

O'Brien explained the Party's philosophy with chilling clarity. There was no objective reality. Reality existed only in the human mind — and the Party controlled the human mind. Therefore, the Party controlled reality.

"Do you begin to see, then, what kind of world we are creating?" O'Brien said. "It is the exact opposite of the stupid hedonistic Utopias that the old reformers imagined. A world of fear and treachery and torment. If you want a picture of the future, imagine a boot stamping on a human face — forever."

Winston broke. He confessed to everything — assassination, sabotage, espionage, selling military secrets. He confessed to crimes he had not committed and crimes that were impossible to commit. He wept, he groveled, he begged.

But one thing remained. In the deepest corner of his mind, he still loved Julia. He had confessed everything — betrayed everyone — but he had not betrayed her. Not in his heart.

O'Brien knew it. "You have held out well, Winston," he said. "Everything else you have betrayed. But there is one person you have not betrayed."

"You have not betrayed Julia," O'Brien said.

Winston was taken to Room 101. It was the room where every prisoner faced their worst fear — the thing they could not endure. For Winston, it was rats.

They brought a cage fitted to his face. Inside were two starving rats. When the door opened, they would eat through his face. Winston could see them, hear them, smell them.

"Do it to Julia!" Winston screamed. "Do it to Julia! Not me! Julia! I don't care what you do to her. Tear her face off, strip her to the bones. Not me! Julia! Not me!"

It was over. The last piece of Winston Smith that was human — his love for Julia — was gone. He had betrayed her not just in words but in his heart. He had wanted them to hurt her instead of him. The Party had reached his soul.

7.title: Chapter 7: The Chestnut Tree
7.text:
They released him. Winston sat in the Chestnut Tree Cafe, drinking Victory Gin. He was a free man — in the way that a man with no self, no beliefs, and no love is free. His face was healed, his body fattened. He had been given a meaningless job that paid a meaningless salary. He was waiting, as all broken men wait, for the bullet.

He saw Julia once. They met by chance in the park. She had changed — thicker, stiffer, with a long scar across her forehead. They sat on a bench together, barely looking at each other.

"I betrayed you," she said.

"I betrayed you," he said.

"Sometimes," she said, "they threaten you with something — something you can't stand up to, can't even think about. And then you say, 'Don't do it to me, do it to somebody else.' And after that, you don't feel the same towards the other person any longer."

"No," Winston said, "you don't feel the same."

They agreed to meet again, but both knew they wouldn't. There was nothing left between them. The Party had done its work completely. Love — the one thing that was supposed to be indestructible, the one thing Julia had sworn they couldn't touch — had been destroyed as thoroughly as a boot crushes an ant.

Winston spent his days at the Chestnut Tree. He traced "2 + 2 = 5" in the dust on the table. He played chess. He drank. Sometimes tears rolled down his face for no reason.

He thought about his mother. She had disappeared in one of the great purges of the fifties. He remembered now — or thought he remembered — a moment from his childhood when he had stolen his little sister's chocolate ration and run away, and when he came back, both his mother and sister were gone. Had they been taken to a camp? Had they died? He would never know.

The telescreen announced a great victory in Africa. Oceania had won a decisive battle against Eurasia. Winston looked up at the enormous face of Big Brother on the screen.

8.title: Chapter 8: He Loved Big Brother
8.text:
The news of the victory in Africa filled the cafe. People cheered. Winston cheered with them. A sense of triumph swept through him, powerful and irrational.

He gazed up at the enormous face. Forty years it had taken him to learn what kind of smile was hidden beneath the dark moustache. O cruel, needless misunderstanding! O stubborn, self-willed exile from the loving breast!

Two gin-scented tears trickled down the sides of his nose. But it was all right, everything was all right. The struggle was finished.

He loved Big Brother.

The telescreen went on talking about the victory. The figures of production, the statistics of triumph — it all blended into a single hymn of praise. The war would continue. The shortages would continue. The lies would continue. Nothing would change.

But Winston did not think about these things anymore. The capacity for independent thought — the thing that had made him dangerous, the thing that had made him human — was gone. Not suppressed, not hidden, but genuinely destroyed. The Party had won.

O'Brien had told him the truth. The Party was not interested in the overt act. It was interested in the thought behind it. Other tyrannies had been content with obedience; the Party demanded love. Other states had destroyed the body; the Party first destroyed the mind, then the body.

They would shoot him eventually. He knew that. One day, walking down a corridor, a bullet would enter the back of his skull. He would simply cease to exist. And in that final moment, his love for Big Brother would be total, genuine, unforced.

The diary was gone. The room above the shop was gone. Julia was gone. The coral paperweight was gone. The golden country of his dreams — the landscape with the stream and the trees and the girl throwing off her clothes — was gone. Memory itself was unreliable. Perhaps it had always been this way. Perhaps he had always loved Big Brother.

Two plus two equals five.

The clocks struck thirteen.

And in the Ministry of Truth, a clerk — perhaps even Winston's replacement — was busy at work, altering the records of the past to match the present, making certain that Oceania had always been at war with Eastasia, that Big Brother had always predicted the victory in Africa, that nothing had ever been different from the way it was now.

The past was dead. The future was unimaginable. And the present was eternal.

He loved Big Brother.

1.title: Chapter 1: Down the Rabbit Hole
1.text:
Alice was beginning to get very tired of sitting by her sister on the bank, and of having nothing to do: once or twice she had peeped into the book her sister was reading, but it had no pictures or conversations in it, and what is the use of a book without pictures or conversations?

So she was considering in her own mind — as well as she could, for the hot day made her feel very sleepy and stupid — whether the pleasure of making a daisy-chain would be worth the trouble of getting up and picking the daisies, when suddenly a White Rabbit with pink eyes ran close by her.

There was nothing so very remarkable in that; nor did Alice think it so very much out of the way to hear the Rabbit say to itself, "Oh dear! Oh dear! I shall be too late!" But when the Rabbit actually took a watch out of its waistcoat-pocket, and looked at it, and then hurried on, Alice started to her feet, for it flashed across her mind that she had never before seen a rabbit with either a waistcoat-pocket, or a watch to take out of it.

Burning with curiosity, she ran across the field after it, and was just in time to see it pop down a large rabbit-hole under the hedge. In another moment down went Alice after it, never once considering how in the world she was to get out again.

The rabbit-hole went straight on like a tunnel for some way, and then dipped suddenly down, so suddenly that Alice had not a moment to think about stopping herself before she found herself falling down what seemed a very deep well. Either the well was very deep, or she fell very slowly, for she had plenty of time as she went down to look about her and to wonder what was going to happen next.

Down, down, down. Would the fall never come to an end? She looked at the sides of the well and noticed that they were filled with cupboards and book-shelves; here and there she saw maps and pictures hung upon pegs. She took down a jar from one of the shelves as she passed; it was labeled "ORANGE MARMALADE," but to her great disappointment it was empty.

At last she reached the bottom and found herself in a long, low hall lit by a row of lamps hanging from the roof. There were doors all round the hall, but they were all locked. Behind a little curtain she discovered a tiny golden key and a door about fifteen inches high. Through the door she could see a beautiful garden with bright flowers and cool fountains — but she could not even get her head through the doorway.

On the glass table she found a little bottle labeled "DRINK ME." After satisfying herself that it was not marked "poison," Alice ventured to taste it, and found it had a most curious flavor — cherry-tart, custard, pine-apple, roast turkey, toffee, and hot buttered toast all at once. She shrank down until she was only ten inches high. Now she could fit through the door — but she had left the golden key on the table far above her head.

She found a small cake marked "EAT ME." She ate it. And then — curiouser and curiouser — she began to grow, and grow, and grow, until her head touched the ceiling and she had to kneel down on the floor.

2.title: Chapter 2: The Pool of Tears
2.text:
"Curiouser and curiouser!" cried Alice, now nine feet tall with her chin resting on her hands on the ceiling. She was so enormous she could barely reach the little golden key on the glass table, and the tiny door to the beautiful garden was more hopeless than ever.

Poor Alice sat down and began to cry. She cried so much that a large pool of tears formed all around her, several inches deep, stretching nearly to the middle of the hall. "You ought to be ashamed of yourself," she said to herself, "a great girl like you crying like this!" But she went on all the same, shedding gallons of tears until there was a large pool all round her, about four inches deep and reaching half down the hall.

After a time, she heard a little pattering of feet in the distance and hastily dried her eyes. It was the White Rabbit, splendidly dressed, with a pair of white kid gloves in one hand and a large fan in the other. He was muttering to himself, "Oh! the Duchess, the Duchess! Oh! won't she be savage if I've kept her waiting!"

Alice took the fan and gloves from the startled Rabbit and began fanning herself. As she fanned, she shrank smaller and smaller. She had just time to notice this before she slipped into the pool of her own tears.

In the pool she encountered a Mouse swimming nearby. Alice tried to make conversation, mentioning her cat Dinah and how brilliant she was at catching mice and birds. The Mouse bristled with offense, and Alice realized she ought to choose her topics more carefully in this strange place.

A collection of curious creatures had by this time gathered in the pool: a Duck, a Dodo, an Eaglet, and several others. Together they all swam to the shore, a most peculiar and bedraggled party, and the great question was how to get dry again.

"The best thing to get us dry," said the Dodo at last, "would be a Caucus-race." It marked out a race-course in a sort of circle and everyone ran when they felt like it and stopped when they liked, so that it was not easy to know when the race was over. When they had run half an hour or so, the Dodo suddenly called out "The race is over!" and everyone crowded round, panting and asking "But who has won?"

The Dodo declared that everyone had won, and that all must have prizes. Alice was obliged to distribute her own comfits as prizes, and the Dodo presented her with her own thimble as her prize, and all the creatures clapped and cheered. It was all very confusing and wonderful and strange.

3.title: Chapter 3: A Mad Tea Party
3.text:
There was a table set out under a tree in front of the house, and the March Hare and the Hatter were having tea at it: a Dormouse was sitting between them, fast asleep, and the other two were using it as a cushion, resting their elbows on it and talking over its head.

"No room! No room!" they cried out when they saw Alice coming.

"There's plenty of room!" said Alice indignantly, and she sat down in a large arm-chair at one end of the table.

"Have some wine," the March Hare said encouragingly.

Alice looked all round the table, but there was nothing on it but tea. "I don't see any wine," she remarked.

"There isn't any," said the March Hare.

"Then it wasn't very civil of you to offer it," said Alice angrily.

"It wasn't very civil of you to sit down without being invited," said the March Hare.

The Hatter had been looking at Alice for some time with great curiosity. "Your hair wants cutting," he said at last.

"You should learn not to make personal remarks," Alice said with some severity. "It's very rude."

The Hatter opened his eyes very wide on hearing this; but all he said was, "Why is a raven like a writing-desk?"

"I believe I can guess that," Alice said eagerly.

"Do you mean you think you can find out the answer?" said the March Hare.

"Exactly so," said Alice.

"Then you should say what you mean," the March Hare went on.

"I do," Alice hastily replied; "at least — at least I mean what I say — that's the same thing, you know."

"Not the same thing a bit!" said the Hatter. "Why, you might just as well say that 'I see what I eat' is the same thing as 'I eat what I see'!"

The Hatter explained the mystery of why it was always six o'clock — always tea-time. He had quarreled with Time, and ever since then, Time had refused to budge. It was always six o'clock; always tea-time; always time for tea but never for washing up. So they moved round the table, using the clean cups.

"Have you guessed the riddle yet?" the Hatter asked, turning to Alice again.

"No, I give up," Alice replied. "What's the answer?"

"I haven't the slightest idea," said the Hatter.

"Nor I," said the March Hare.

Alice sighed wearily. "I think you might do something better with the time than wasting it in asking riddles that have no answers."

The Dormouse had fallen asleep again, and the others were trying to stuff it into the teapot. Alice got up in disgust and walked away. "It's the stupidest tea-party I ever was at in all my life!" she declared. But she looked back once or twice, and the Hatter and March Hare were trying to put the Dormouse into the teapot, fast asleep.

4.title: Chapter 4: The Queen's Croquet Ground
4.text:
A large rose-tree stood near the entrance of the garden, and the roses growing on it were white, but there were three gardeners at it, busily painting them red. Alice thought this a very curious thing, and she went nearer to watch them.

"Would you tell me," said Alice timidly, "why you are painting those roses?"

Five and Seven said nothing, but looked at Two. Two began in a low voice, "Why, the fact is, you see, Miss, this here ought to have been a red rose-tree, and we put a white one in by mistake; and if the Queen was to find it out, we should all have our heads cut off."

At this moment Five shouted out "The Queen! The Queen!" and the three gardeners instantly threw themselves flat upon their faces. A great procession came — soldiers, courtiers, royal children, and at last the Queen of Hearts herself, magnificent and terrible.

"Who are these?" said the Queen, pointing to the gardeners who were lying on their faces.

"How should I know?" said Alice, surprised at her own courage. "It's no business of mine."

The Queen turned crimson with fury, and after glaring at Alice for a moment, began screaming, "Off with her head! Off with—"

"Nonsense!" said Alice, very loudly and decidedly, and the Queen was silent.

The Queen invited Alice to play croquet. It was a most peculiar game — the croquet balls were live hedgehogs, the mallets were live flamingoes, and the soldiers had to double themselves up to make the arches. Alice found it very difficult to manage her flamingo; the moment she thought she had it nicely straightened out, it would twist itself round and look up in her face with such a puzzled expression that she could not help bursting out laughing.

The Queen dashed about, screaming "Off with his head!" or "Off with her head!" at least once a minute. The players all played at once without waiting for turns, quarreling all the while and fighting for the hedgehogs. The Cheshire Cat's grinning face appeared in the air and Alice talked to it, grateful for a familiar presence in this chaos. The Cat asked how she was getting on. Alice said the Queen was "most dreadfully savage" — and the Queen, overhearing, demanded the Cat's head be cut off, only to discover the head had no body, and no one knew quite how to proceed.

5.title: Chapter 5: The Mock Turtle's Story
5.text:
"You've no idea how glad I am to see you again, you dear old thing!" said the Duchess, tucking her arm affectionately into Alice's as they walked off together. The Duchess seemed in a surprisingly pleasant humor now, without the pepper that had been in the kitchen before.

The Queen had asked Alice to go and visit the Mock Turtle, to hear his history. She found the Mock Turtle and the Gryphon sitting together on a ledge of rock, side by side. The Mock Turtle looked at them with large eyes full of tears, but said nothing.

"This here young lady," said the Gryphon to the Mock Turtle, "she wants for to know your history, she do."

"I'll tell it her," said the Mock Turtle in a deep, hollow tone. "Sit down, both of you, and don't speak a word till I've finished."

So they sat down, and nobody spoke for some minutes. Alice thought to herself, "I don't see how he can ever finish, if he doesn't begin." But she waited patiently.

"Once," said the Mock Turtle at last, with a deep sigh, "I was a real Turtle."

These words were followed by a very long silence. "When we were little," the Mock Turtle went on at last, more calmly, "we went to school in the sea. The master was an old Turtle — we used to call him Tortoise—"

"Why did you call him Tortoise, if he wasn't one?" Alice asked.

"We called him Tortoise because he taught us," said the Mock Turtle angrily. "Really you ought to be ashamed of yourself for asking such a simple question."

"We had the best of educations," the Mock Turtle continued. "Reeling and Writhing, of course, to begin with, and then the different branches of Arithmetic — Ambition, Distraction, Uglification, and Derision."

"I never heard of Uglification," Alice ventured.

"What is the use of Repetition?" the Gryphon demanded.

The Mock Turtle described the ten hours of lessons — less every day, ten the first, nine the next, and so on, which was why they were called lessons, because they lessened from day to day. He spoke of the dances — the Lobster Quadrille — with such longing and sorrow that Alice almost felt she could see the great waves of the sea and hear the distant sound of the lobsters being thrown into the ocean.

"Shall we try the Lobster Quadrille?" the Gryphon asked eagerly. The Mock Turtle sighed deeply, "I was a real Turtle once." And they danced, slowly and sadly, at the edge of the sea.

6.title: Chapter 6: Who Stole the Tarts?
6.text:
The King and Queen of Hearts were seated on their throne when Alice arrived with the Gryphon. All the animals and creatures of Wonderland were gathered before them. The Knave of Hearts stood in the dock, bound with chains, and before him was a great dish of tarts. Alice had seen those tarts before — golden and jam-filled, gleaming on a white cloth — and she felt certain this was a very important occasion.

The charge was read aloud by the White Rabbit in his squeaky voice: the Knave of Hearts had stolen some tarts, all on a summer's day.

"Consider your verdict," the King said to the jury.

"Not yet, not yet!" the Rabbit hastily interrupted. "There's a great deal to come before that!"

Alice looked at the jury-box. Twelve creatures were busily writing on their slates. Bill the Lizard was there, and a little frog, and several others she half recognized. She noticed that if a juror didn't understand something, the others would all write it down anyway.

Alice began to feel very curious about herself. She had been growing, quietly and steadily, ever since she sat down. She stretched her neck over the heads of the jury and found she could see everything quite clearly from her great height.

The first witness was the Hatter, who came in with a teacup in one hand and a piece of bread-and-butter in the other. He had not finished his tea when he was summoned.

"Take off your hat," the King said.

"It isn't mine," said the Hatter.

"Stolen!" said the King, and looked at the jury. The jury eagerly wrote this down on their slates.

"I keep them to sell," the Hatter added. "I've none of my own. I'm a hatter."

The King asked him to give his evidence. The Hatter began, twisting his hat round and round nervously, and at one point taking a bite of his bread-and-butter. He could not quite remember what had happened. The Dormouse was called next and fell asleep immediately. The Queen threatened to have him suppressed.

The White Rabbit called out "Silence in the court!" and read out a curious paper with verses on it. "That's the most important piece of evidence we've heard yet," said the King. Alice was not so sure. She was growing again, she could feel it, and the courtroom felt smaller and stranger around her.

7.title: Chapter 7: Alice's Evidence
7.text:
"Alice!" called the White Rabbit in a shrill, eager tone. "Alice is next to give evidence!"

"Here!" cried Alice, and in her eagerness she quite forgot how large she had grown in the last few minutes, and she jumped up in such a hurry that she tipped over the jury-box with the edge of her skirt, upsetting all the jurymen onto the heads of the crowd below.

"Oh, I beg your pardon!" she exclaimed in a tone of great dismay, and began picking them up again as quickly as she could.

"The trial cannot proceed," said the King in a very grave voice, "until all the jurymen are back in their proper places — all," he repeated with great emphasis, looking hard at Alice as he said so.

Alice looked at the jury-box, and saw she had put the Lizard in head downwards, and the poor little thing was waving its tail about in a melancholy way, being quite unable to move. She soon got it out again, and put it right.

"What do you know about this business?" the King said to Alice.

"Nothing," said Alice.

"Nothing whatever?" persisted the King.

"Nothing whatever," said Alice.

"That's very important," the King said, turning to the jury. They were just beginning to write this down on their slates, when the White Rabbit interrupted, "Unimportant, your Majesty means, of course."

"Unimportant, of course, I meant," the King hastily said, and went on to himself in an undertone, "important — unimportant — unimportant — important —" as if he were trying which word sounded best.

"Rule Forty-two," announced the King suddenly. "All persons more than a mile high to leave the court."

"I'm not a mile high," said Alice.

"You are," said the King.

"Nearly two miles high," added the Queen.

"Well, I shan't go, at any rate," said Alice. "Besides, that's not a regular rule. You invented it just now."

"It's the oldest rule in the book," said the King.

"Then it ought to be Number One," said Alice.

The Queen had meanwhile been staring at Alice with growing fury. She turned to the jury: "Sentence first — verdict afterwards."

"Stuff and nonsense!" said Alice loudly. "The idea of having the sentence first!"

"Hold your tongue!" said the Queen, turning purple.

"I won't!" said Alice.

"Off with her head!" the Queen shouted at the top of her voice. Nobody moved.

"Who cares for you?" said Alice — she had grown to her full size now. "You're nothing but a pack of cards!"

At this the whole pack rose up into the air and came flying down upon her: she gave a little scream, half of fright and half of anger, and tried to beat them off, and found herself lying on the bank, with her head in her sister's lap.

8.title: Chapter 8: Awakening
8.text:
Alice's sister gently brushed away some dead leaves that had fluttered down from the trees upon her face. "Wake up, Alice dear!" said her sister. "Why, what a long sleep you've had!"

"Oh, I've had such a curious dream!" said Alice, and she told her sister, as well as she could remember them, all these strange Adventures of hers that you have just been reading about. When she had finished, her sister kissed her and said, "It was a curious dream, dear, certainly: but now run in to your tea; it's getting late."

So Alice got up and ran off, thinking while she ran — as well she might — what a wonderful dream it had been.

But her sister sat still just as she had left her, leaning her head on her hand, watching the setting sun, and thinking of little Alice and all her wonderful Adventures, till she too began dreaming after a fashion.

She imagined little Alice sitting with her knees drawn up and her eyes eager and bright — listening to a tale, perhaps — and how the tale grew strangely as she listened, the simple story stretching into strange and wonderful shapes.

She pictured all the strange creatures she had heard of: the White Rabbit with his watch, hurrying always and never arriving; the curious pool of Alice's own tears; the Mouse so easily offended; the Dodo and his Caucus-race; the blue Caterpillar sitting on his mushroom and smoking his hookah; the Cheshire Cat fading to nothing but its smile; the Mad Hatter and his eternal tea party where time stood still at six o'clock; the Queen shrieking "Off with their heads!" at everyone who crossed her; the Mock Turtle sighing about the lessons that lessened; and the cards rising up in a great fluttering storm.

Last of all, she pictured her little sister in years to come, keeping the simple and loving heart of her childhood: gathering other little children about her, making their eyes bright and eager with many a strange tale, perhaps even with the dream of Wonderland of long ago.

And Alice did grow up, as children must. But in her heart she kept that curious feeling — the sense that the world was stranger and more wonderful than it appeared, that a rabbit in a waistcoat was worth following, that a door fifteen inches high might open into the most beautiful garden imaginable, and that the best questions were the ones nobody could answer. She kept it always: the memory of falling, and of a world where nothing was impossible, and where the greatest magic of all was simply the curiosity of a child who was not yet afraid to wonder.

1.title: Chapter 1: Jonathan Harker's Journal
1.text:
3 May. Bistritz. — Left Munich at 8:35 P.M., on 1st May, arriving at Vienna early next morning. Budapest seems a wonderful place, from the glimpse which I got of it from the train and the little I could walk through the streets. I feared to go very far from the station, as we had arrived late and would start as near the correct time as possible.

The impression I had was that we were leaving the West and entering the East; the most Western of splendid bridges over the Danube, which is here of noble width and depth, took us among the traditions of Turkish rule.

I read that every known superstition in the world is gathered into the horseshoe of the Carpathians, as if it were the centre of some sort of imaginative whirlpool. I was told that the driver would not take me all the way to the castle — that the Borgo Pass was as far as any local man would venture after dark.

At the Bistritz hotel, I found a letter waiting for me from Count Dracula himself: "My Friend, Welcome to the Carpathians. I am anxiously expecting you. Sleep well tonight. At three tomorrow the diligence will start for Bukovina; a place on it is kept for you. At the Borgo Pass my carriage will await you and will bring you to me. I trust that your journey from London has been a happy one, and that you will enjoy your stay in my beautiful land." The letter was signed simply, "Dracula."

The landlady was very distressed when I told her my destination. She crossed herself and begged me not to go. When I told her that I must, she placed a crucifix around my neck, pressing it into my hands with such force that it left marks on my fingers.

The coach ride into the mountains was wild and beautiful and terrifying. The other passengers whispered among themselves and stared at me with pitying eyes. As the clock struck midnight, a solitary carriage appeared from the darkness, drawn by four coal-black horses. The driver — tall, with a long brown beard and a great black hat — said nothing as I climbed in.

At last I saw before me the Castle Dracula, outlined against the moonlit sky: a vast ruined castle, from whose tall black windows came no ray of light, and whose broken battlements showed a jagged line against the sky. The door was great and old, all studded with large iron nails, and set in a projecting doorway of massive stone. I could find no bell or knocker.

Then the great door swung open, and an old man stood in the opening. He was clad in black from head to foot, without a single speck of colour about him anywhere. His face was strong — aquiline, with high bridge of the thin nose and peculiarly arched nostrils. His eyebrows were massive, almost meeting over the nose. The mouth was fixed and cruel-looking, with peculiarly sharp white teeth that protruded over the lips. His ears were pale and at the tops extremely pointed.

"Welcome to my house," he said. "Enter freely and of your own will!" He extended his hand, and I took it. It was as cold as ice — more like the hand of a dead than a living man. "Welcome to my house. Come freely. Go safely; and leave something of the happiness you bring."

I was Jonathon Harker, English solicitor, come to complete a legal transaction concerning property in London. But standing in the doorway of Castle Dracula, hearing the wolves howl in the mountains below, I felt the first chill of something I could not yet name.

2.title: Chapter 2: The Prisoner
2.text:
I have been here a week now, and though the Count has been gracious — discussing English law, London society, history — I have begun to observe things which fill me with unease.

There are no servants in the house. The Count himself carries my luggage, lays the table, makes my bed. No mirrors anywhere — when I produced my small travelling mirror to shave, Dracula appeared behind me and I saw only my own reflection. He seized the mirror and flung it from the window, smashing it in the courtyard below. "I have no reflection," he said softly, as if explaining something quite natural. "You will find, Mr. Harker, that such vanities become meaningless in time."

I noticed his hands at breakfast — long, thin fingers with hair growing in the centres of the palms, and nails curved and cut to a sharp point like a bird's claw. When his hand brushed mine, I shuddered.

One morning I attempted to leave the castle by the front door. It was locked. I searched the entire eastern wing — every window barred, every door bolted. The castle is a prison, and I am its prisoner.

That same night, unable to sleep, I crept to a window in the south wall and looked out. In the moonlight, I saw the Count emerge from a window below me and begin to crawl face-down along the castle wall, his cloak spreading around him like great wings. His fingers and toes gripped the stone as though they were made of it. Down and down he went, moving with the ease of a lizard, until he disappeared into the darkness below.

What manner of man is this? What manner of creature in the semblance of man?

Three days later, I was writing in the Count's library when I heard a soft sound behind me — a low, sweet laughter. Three young women stood in the moonlight streaming through the tall windows. Two were dark, with great dark piercing eyes. The third was fair, with great wavy masses of golden hair and eyes like pale sapphires. They were all three voluptuous, and the two dark ones had high aquiline noses. They looked at me with longing, and I felt in my heart a wicked burning desire that they would kiss me.

The fair girl advanced and bent over me. I could feel the hot breath on my neck — then the soft, shivering touch of lips, and two sharp points of the teeth touching and pausing. I closed my eyes in surrender.

Then the Count was in the room. He seized the fair woman by the throat and flung her back with a strength that sent her crashing into the far wall. "How dare you touch him!" he snarled, his eyes blazing red. "This man belongs to me!"

I fainted. When I woke, I was alone. But I knew then, with terrible certainty, that I would never leave Castle Dracula alive by ordinary means. I began to keep this journal carefully, hiding it in my coat — for if I escape, the world must know what walks these mountains. And if I do not escape, perhaps someone will find it and understand.

3.title: Chapter 3: Mina and Lucy
3.text:
Mina Murray's Journal. Whitby, 24 July. — Lucy met me at the station, looking sweeter and lovelier than ever, and we drove up to the house at the Crescent, in which they have rooms. This is a lovely place. The houses of the old town are all red-roofed, and those on the east side are packed closely on the hillside, tier above tier, like the back row of a theatre.

Lucy is as sweet as ever. She tells me she has had three proposals of marriage in one day — three! First from Dr. Jack Seward, who runs the great lunatic asylum at Purfleet. He is a fine young man, and brave and strong. Then from Mr. Quincey Morris, a Texan with bright eyes and a drawling voice. And finally from her true love, Arthur Holmwood, the son of Lord Godalming. She accepted Arthur, of course, and she is radiant with happiness.

I am worried about my own Jonathan. His last letter was weeks ago, from Bistritz in Transylvania. Since then, nothing. I try not to think of the dark things.

26 July. — We have met a most interesting old man, Mr. Swales, who sits on the bench near the ruined abbey and tells us the local legends. He laughs at the superstitions of the graveyard. "These be all just bogie tales," he says. But there is a sadness behind his laughter, and he points sometimes to a fresh-looking grave and falls silent.

The old graveyard on the cliff is a strange and lovely place. Below us, the harbour with its twin piers. On the other side, the ruined abbey of Whitby with its arched windows and crumbling walls. Lucy loves to sit here at night and watch the sea. I find it peaceful, but lately I have heard strange noises in the dark — a sort of flapping and scratching that I cannot account for.

3 August. — Strange ships have been passing. The weather has turned foul, and there are mutterings among the sailors on the quay about ill omens. Lucy has been walking in her sleep again, something she has not done since childhood. I found her last night at the window in her nightgown, looking out at the abbey on the cliff.

I must watch her more carefully. She looked pale this morning, and there were shadows under her eyes. She said she heard someone calling her name in the night — a voice she could not resist. When I asked her what it sounded like, she closed her eyes and shuddered pleasantly. "Like music," she said. "Like the most beautiful music you have ever heard."

I do not know why this answer frightened me so.

4.title: Chapter 4: The Demeter
4.text:
Cutting from the Dailygraph, 8 August. — A coastguard reported that during the height of last night's storm, a great ship came crashing through the harbour mouth under full sail, though no hand was at the wheel. She ran straight for the beach under Tate Hill Pier and grounded herself there. When the coastguard boarded her, they found the wheel lashed and a dead man tied to it — the captain, his hands gripped around the spokes, his face frozen in an expression of absolute terror.

The ship was the Russian schooner Demeter, out of Varna. No crew were found — only the captain, dead, and the ship's log, which told a terrible story of a voyage from the Black Sea in which the crew had dwindled one by one, lost overboard in circumstances that the captain could not explain, though he suspected the great box of earth in the hold.

As the ship struck, a huge dog was seen to leap from the deck and bound away over the sands and up the cliff toward the ruined abbey. Despite extensive searching, the animal was never found.

Mina Murray's Journal. — That same night, Lucy walked in her sleep again. I followed her from our room, out through the streets, and up the long steps to the graveyard on the cliff. She was seated on a bench near the old tombstone, her white nightgown bright in the moonlight. Bending over her was a tall, dark figure — a man, I thought at first, though I could not see his face. He raised his head as I called Lucy's name, and I saw something white against Lucy's throat.

I ran forward calling her name. When I reached her she was alone, no figure anywhere to be seen. She was cold and barely conscious. I wrapped my cloak around her and guided her home. In the morning, I examined her throat and found two small puncture marks — tiny, as if made by a large pin. She did not remember anything.

For days afterward, Lucy grew pale and weak. She slept late and was drowsy through the day, but at night she was restless, always drifting to her window. The puncture marks on her throat seemed not to heal. I wrote to Dr. Seward, describing Lucy's symptoms, begging him to come.

The answer came back: he would come at once, and he was bringing someone — a professor from Amsterdam, the greatest authority in Europe on obscure diseases. His name was Van Helsing.

5.title: Chapter 5: Dr. Van Helsing
5.text:
Dr. Seward's Diary. — Prof. Van Helsing arrived yesterday by the night train. He is a seemingly arbitrary man, but with a mighty brain and an iron resolution. He is a philosopher and a metaphysician, and one of the most advanced scientists of his day. He has an absolutely open mind, which is perhaps the most valuable quality a man can have.

He examined Lucy in silence for a long time. He lifted her eyelids, pressed her hands, placed his ear to her chest. Then he stepped back and looked at Seward with an expression that, for just a moment, was not that of a scientist at all, but of a frightened man.

"Friend John," he said quietly, "this case is one that I have not met with before. Tell me — have you noticed anything unusual about the room? Any odours? Any marks on the window?"

He produced from his bag a quantity of white garlic flowers and hung them around the room — festooned across the windows, coiled around the door frame, draped like a necklace about Lucy's throat. Lucy's mother, thinking this was some strange foreign medicine, removed them all the next morning while Lucy slept.

That night Lucy nearly died.

"You do not understand," Van Helsing told us, and his voice was terrible with controlled urgency. "The garlic is not a medicine. It is a protection. There are forces at work here that your medical school did not teach you — that mine did not teach me, until I went looking in very old books. You must trust me absolutely. We must replace the garlic tonight."

Lucy's condition worsened despite our efforts. We arranged for blood transfusions — Seward gave blood, then Quincey Morris, then Van Helsing himself. The colour would return to her cheeks, her breathing would ease — and then in the morning she would be pale and weak again, as if the blood had drained away in the night.

We took turns watching at her door. Van Helsing produced crucifixes and made each of us carry one. He would not explain why, except to say: "There are things in this world which are older than we know, and which do not follow the rules that we have made for nature."

One morning we found Lucy worse than ever, her face like white marble, the marks on her throat red and raw. Her mother had died suddenly of heart failure two nights before — the shock of it, perhaps. Or something else. Van Helsing sat beside the bed and held Lucy's cold hand and said nothing for a very long time.

She died that evening, just before sunset. Her face in death was very peaceful, and very beautiful — more beautiful, somehow, than she had been in life. It troubled me. It troubled Van Helsing most of all. He leaned over and examined her throat, and I heard him say very softly, as if to himself, "She is not yet dead. She is Un-Dead." He would not explain what he meant.

6.title: Chapter 6: The Un-Dead
6.text:
Dr. Seward's Diary. — There have been strange reports from Hampstead Heath. Children have gone missing at night and returned in the morning with small wounds on their throats, speaking of a "beautiful lady" who had taken them into the dark to play. Van Helsing read the newspaper account and said nothing, but his face was grim.

He asked me to come with him to Lucy's tomb. I thought he meant to pay our respects. Instead, he brought a bag filled with tools — a crowbar, a wooden stake, a hammer — and a wreath of garlic flowers, and a crucifix of silver.

"What I am going to tell you is madness," he said, as we stood before the crypt in the cold night air. "Or rather, it will seem so. But you have seen with your own eyes things that cannot be explained by science alone. I ask you only to look, and to remember what you see."

He opened the tomb. Lucy's coffin was empty.

We waited in the darkness of the crypt for nearly an hour. Then I heard a sound — a soft, melodic humming — and into the moonlight came a figure in white. It was Lucy. Or the thing that had been Lucy. She was beautiful still, but the beauty was different now — cold and terrible and hungry. In her arms she carried a sleeping child.

She looked at us. She smiled. "Come to me, Arthur," she said, and her voice was like dark honey. "My arms are hungry for you. Come, and we can rest together." Her lips were red — wet and red — and when she opened her mouth I saw the white teeth and the pointed canines.

Arthur Holmwood had followed us. He stepped forward, drawn by her voice. Van Helsing interposed himself and thrust the crucifix forward. Lucy recoiled with a shriek that was nothing human.

We returned in the morning, with Arthur. Van Helsing explained what must be done, and why — for her soul's sake, not merely to stop the terror. Arthur was the one who drove the stake, because he had loved her most. What happened then I will not describe in detail. But when it was done, and we looked at Lucy's face in the coffin, she was at peace — truly at peace, the beauty returned to what it had been in life, the cruel lines gone.

We stood over her for a long moment in silence. Then Van Helsing spoke.

"She is at rest now. But there is still the one who made her so. The vampire — the Count — he is here in London. He has brought his boxes of earth from Transylvania, his lairs where he can sleep and grow strong. We must find them all, and destroy them. And we must do it before he takes another."

"Then we are at war," said Quincey Morris.

"Yes," said Van Helsing. "We are at war."

7.title: Chapter 7: The Hunt
7.text:
Mina Harker's Journal. — Jonathan is home. He was found in a convent hospital in Budapest, delirious with brain fever, and the kind Sisters nursed him back to health. He remembers little of his time in Transylvania, and what he does remember, he is not certain is real. I married him at his bedside, and brought him home to England.

He is changed. Thinner. Older in his eyes. Sometimes at night he cries out in his sleep, and I hold him and ask him nothing.

It was a letter in the newspaper that broke through his amnesia — a notice of the death of Count Dracula's solicitor in Exeter. Jonathan went white as paper. "He is here," he said. "He came. I helped him come." And then he told me everything.

I brought his journal to Van Helsing. I had already begun to suspect, from everything that had happened with Lucy, that the threads connected. Van Helsing read every word and sat for a long time in silence. "Madam Mina," he said at last, "you are the most remarkable woman I have ever met. And your husband's account is true in every particular. The Count is here, and we must find him."

We formed our band of hunters: Van Helsing, Dr. Seward, Arthur Holmwood, Quincey Morris, Jonathan, and myself. We pooled our information, plotted the locations of Dracula's properties across London — Carfax Abbey near the asylum, a house in Piccadilly, others in Bermondsey and Mile End. In each, he had stored boxes of Transylvanian earth, his required resting places.

We raided them one by one, sterilising the earth in each box with the Host. We found forty-nine boxes in total. We needed to find the last one.

Then one night — I woke to find the Count in my room. He had come through the window, which was locked, and Jonathan lay beside me in an unnatural sleep. The Count's eyes were red with fury and with something else — satisfaction. "Your husband sleeps," he said pleasantly. "You will not wake him by screaming." And he forced me to drink from the wound in his chest, while I struggled and could not stop myself, and the men burst in too late.

"Now you are mine," he said as they drove him back with the crucifix and he dissolved into mist before their eyes. "You bear my mark. You will call to me, and I will call to you. You cannot hide from me, and you cannot truly work against me."

In the morning, when Van Helsing touched the consecrated wafer to my forehead to bless me, it burned like fire. I wept, and Jonathan held me, and I could see in his eyes the thing I could not bear — that he blamed himself for everything.

Dracula had been loading his remaining boxes onto a ship. He was fleeing back to Transylvania, to his castle, to safety. We had only one advantage: through our telepathic link, Van Helsing could sometimes read Dracula's location through my mind. We would follow. We would end it at the source.

8.title: Chapter 8: The Final Battle
8.text:
Mina Harker's Journal. — We divided into parties and raced for Transylvania. Some went by sea. Jonathan and I went overland with Van Helsing, following the Count's route through the mountain passes. The days were growing short and the cold was deepening.

Van Helsing hypnotised me at dawn and at dusk, and through me read the Count's position in his box aboard the ship. Moving east. Always east, toward home and safety. If he reached the castle before sunset on any given day, he could bring the earth of his homeland around him and restore his power fully. We dared not let that happen.

We were in the foothills of the Carpathians when Mina had her crisis. In a trance she smiled — a dreadful, alien smile — and whispered directions to the castle as though welcoming us. Van Helsing recognised the sign: Dracula was drawing on the link, trying to use her against us. They rode faster.

Jonathan Harker's Journal, 6 November. — We converged on the castle road as the sun was already touching the peaks. Below us in the mountain pass, a wagon was driving hard through the snow, drawn by straining horses, surrounded by a band of mounted Szgany — gypsies in the Count's employ. Inside the wagon was the last box of earth.

We rode to intercept them. There were guns fired, horses screaming, a confusion of smoke and snow. Quincey Morris was stabbed in the side and went down, but kept moving.

Dr. Seward's Account. — The wagon was stopped. The Szgany, seeing the crucifixes Van Helsing held aloft, fell back in sudden superstitious terror. The box was heaved from the wagon bed. The lid was forced open.

The Count lay inside, his eyes burning with infernal light, his face cruelly triumphant even as the sun fell lower and the shadow of the mountain crept toward him. His hands were at his sides, the long fingers curling. He knew the sunset would restore him. We had seconds.

Jonathan Harker came over the side of the wagon like a man possessed and drove his Kukri knife in a great sweeping arc across the Count's throat. The wound opened to the bone, and in the same instant Quincey Morris, bleeding heavily but upright, drove his Bowie knife home into the Count's heart.

What happened then was swift and strange. The body seemed to crumble — not like a man dying, but like something very ancient finally completing its dissolution. The dust dispersed in the wind that came down from the mountain, and in a moment there was nothing in the box but earth. Jonathan's knife rang on the wood. The Count was gone.

Quincey Morris died at sunset, his hand in Mina's. His last words were that he was glad it was over, that she was free, that the mark had faded from her forehead and her face was at peace. He smiled as the last light left his eyes.

Mina Harker's Journal, Seven Years Later. — We have a boy. Jonathan and I named him Quincey, for the friend who gave his life that all of us might live. He is growing up strong and curious and brave, knowing nothing yet of what his name signifies.

Sometimes I take down the journals from those terrible months and read them through. It seems impossible that it was real — the castle on the mountain, the women in the moonlight, the ship crashing through the harbour in the storm. But the scar on my forehead, though faded, is still there if you know where to look. And Jonathan sometimes wakes in the night and I hold him, and neither of us speaks, and that is enough.

The world is full of dark things. But we faced one of them, and the light held.

1.title: Chapter 1: The Explorer's Discovery
1.text:
Captain Robert Walton was a man consumed by ambition. Aboard his ship in the frozen Arctic, writing letters to his sister Margaret back in England, he confessed his burning desire: to reach the North Pole, to discover what no man had found before.

"I shall satiate my ardent curiosity with the sight of a part of the world never before visited," he wrote. "These are my enticements, and they are sufficient to conquer all fear of danger or death."

But the Arctic was merciless. The ship became trapped in ice. For days they drifted, the crew growing restless. Then, on a morning thick with fog, they saw something extraordinary — a gigantic figure driving a dog-sled across the ice, moving northward at great speed. The figure was immense, far larger than any ordinary man.

The next morning, they found another traveler — a man on a fragment of ice, nearly frozen to death. They brought him aboard. He was emaciated, his limbs nearly destroyed by the cold. But his eyes burned with an intelligence and sorrow that Walton had never seen.

When Walton mentioned the giant figure they had spotted, the stranger's expression changed to one of wild anxiety. "Has the sledge that preceded mine arrived?"

As the stranger recovered, Walton felt an immediate connection. Here was a man of extraordinary mind and deep feeling. The stranger saw in Walton a reflection of his own former ambition, and it horrified him.

"You seek for knowledge and wisdom, as I once did," the stranger said. "I ardently hope that the gratification of your wishes may not be a serpent to sting you, as mine has been."

He agreed to tell his story — a warning, he said, for anyone who would pursue forbidden knowledge.

2.title: Chapter 2: Victor's Obsession
2.text:
The stranger's name was Victor Frankenstein. He was born in Geneva to a loving, wealthy family. His father, Alphonse, was noble and devoted. His mother, Caroline, was beautiful and compassionate. They adopted Elizabeth Lavenza, an orphan of extraordinary grace, who became Victor's closest companion and, he always knew, his future wife.

Victor's childhood was idyllic. He and Elizabeth grew up alongside Henry Clerval, his dearest friend — a boy full of imagination and romantic ideals. But where Clerval dreamed of chivalry and adventure, Victor was drawn to darker questions.

At thirteen, he discovered the works of Cornelius Agrippa — an alchemist who had sought the philosopher's stone and the elixir of life. His father dismissed it as "sad trash," but Victor was captivated. He devoured the writings of Paracelsus and Albertus Magnus, dreaming of conquering death itself.

"Wealth was an inferior object," Victor recalled, "but what glory would attend the discovery, if I could banish disease from the human frame and render man invulnerable to any but a violent death!"

A thunderstorm changed everything. Victor watched lightning strike a tree, reducing it to a thin ribbon of wood. He turned to modern science — electricity, galvanism, the hidden forces of nature. The alchemists' dreams, he realized, might be achievable through real science.

At seventeen, tragedy struck. His mother caught scarlet fever from Elizabeth and died. Her last wish: that Victor and Elizabeth would one day marry.

Grief-stricken, Victor left for the University of Ingolstadt. There, Professor Waldman spoke words that sealed Victor's fate: "The modern masters have indeed performed miracles. They penetrate into the recesses of nature and show how she works in her hiding-places."

Victor threw himself into his studies with terrifying intensity. He became obsessed with one question: what is the principle of life? He haunted charnel-houses and graveyards, studying death to understand life. And after months of feverish labor, he found it.

"I succeeded in discovering the cause of generation and life," he said. "Nay, more, I became myself capable of bestowing animation upon lifeless matter."

He decided to create a human being.

3.title: Chapter 3: The Creation
3.text:
For nearly two years, Victor worked in isolation. He collected materials from charnel-houses, dissecting rooms, and slaughterhouses. His workshop was a solitary chamber at the top of his lodgings — a place that would later haunt his nightmares.

He had chosen to make the creature large — about eight feet tall — because the minuteness of human parts made smaller work impossible. He selected beautiful features: lustrous black hair, teeth of pearly whiteness. But as the work progressed, the beauty he intended became something else entirely.

He neglected everything. He sent no letters home. Elizabeth wrote with increasing worry. Clerval wrote with cheerful news of Geneva. Victor read nothing, answered nothing. His cheeks had grown pale, his body emaciated, his nerves shattered. But he could not stop.

"Who shall conceive the horrors of my secret toil, as I dabbled among the unhallowed damps of the grave?" he recalled. "I seemed to have lost all soul or sensation but for this one pursuit."

On a dreary night of November, it was finished. By the glimmer of a half-extinguished light, Victor saw the dull yellow eye of the creature open. It breathed hard, and a convulsive motion agitated its limbs.

The creature was alive.

And Victor was horrified.

"How can I describe my emotions at this catastrophe?" he cried. "His limbs were in proportion, and I had selected his features as beautiful. Beautiful! Great God! His yellow skin scarcely covered the work of muscles and arteries beneath; his hair was of a lustrous black, and flowing; his teeth of a pearly whiteness; but these luxuriances only formed a more horrid contrast with his watery eyes, his shrivelled complexion and straight black lips."

Victor fled. He ran into the streets, pacing through the night in a state of wild agitation. The thing he had created — the thing he had labored for — was a monster. He could not bear to look at what he had done.

He returned to find the creature gone. Relief flooded through him, and he collapsed into a nervous fever that lasted months. Henry Clerval, who had arrived at Ingolstadt, nursed him back to health — never knowing what Victor had done.

4.title: Chapter 4: The Creature's Story
4.text:
Victor returned to Geneva to find his world shattered. His young brother William had been murdered — strangled in a park. And Justine Moritz, a beloved family servant, was accused of the crime. A locket belonging to William was found in her pocket.

Victor knew the truth the moment he saw a figure lurking near the murder scene during a thunderstorm. It was the creature — enormous, unmistakable. Victor was certain: his creation had killed William. But he could say nothing. Who would believe him?

Justine was convicted and executed. Victor was consumed by guilt. Two people were now dead because of him.

He fled to the Alps, seeking solace in the mountains. On the Mer de Glace, the creature appeared. Victor cursed him, called him a devil. But the creature spoke — and his words were articulate, intelligent, and devastating.

"I was benevolent and good; misery made me a fiend," the creature said. "Make me happy, and I shall again be virtuous."

He told his story. After fleeing Victor's workshop, the creature wandered alone, bewildered by a world he did not understand. He learned to survive — finding fire, seeking shelter. Gradually, he discovered a cottage and, hiding in an adjoining hovel, observed a family: the blind old man De Lacey, his son Felix, and daughter Agatha.

For months, the creature watched them. He learned language from their conversations. He learned to read. He gathered wood for them secretly, becoming their invisible benefactor. He felt love for this family — the first love of his existence.

"My heart yearned to be known and loved by these amiable creatures," the creature recalled. "I dared not think that they would turn from me with disdain and horror."

When he finally revealed himself to the blind old man, there was a moment of hope. De Lacey was kind, listening without prejudice. But when Felix returned and saw the creature, he attacked him violently. The family fled their cottage and never returned.

The creature's heart broke. The one family he loved had rejected him. He found Victor's journal in the pocket of the clothes he had taken from the laboratory. He read his creator's words of horror and disgust.

"Hateful day when I received life!" the creature cried. "Accursed creator! Why did you form a monster so hideous that even you turned from me in disgust?"

Enraged, the creature traveled to Geneva. He found little William and, in a moment of fury, strangled him. He placed the locket on Justine to ensure her destruction.

5.title: Chapter 5: The Creature's Demand
5.text:
The creature made his demand: create a female companion for him. A mate as hideous as himself, so that he would not be alone. In return, he would disappear with her into the wilds of South America, far from all human contact.

"If I have no ties and no affections, hatred and vice must be my portion," the creature declared. "My vices are the children of a forced solitude that I abhor. My virtues will necessarily arise when I live in communion with an equal."

Victor was torn. The request was reasonable — even just. The creature had been abandoned by his creator, rejected by all humanity. He had never asked to be made. He deserved some measure of happiness.

But could Victor trust him? And could he bear to create another such being?

"You swear to be harmless?" Victor demanded.

"I swear," the creature said, "by the sun, and by the blue sky of heaven, that if you grant my prayer, you shall never behold me again."

Reluctantly, Victor agreed. The creature vanished into the mountains.

Victor could not bring himself to begin the work in Geneva, surrounded by his loved ones. He told his father he needed to travel to England — ostensibly for study, but truly to find a remote place to work. Henry Clerval accompanied him.

They traveled through England, visiting Oxford and the Lake District. Clerval was enchanted by everything — the literature, the landscapes, the history. Victor, carrying his terrible secret, could barely look at the beauty around him.

Finally, Victor separated from Clerval and retreated to a desolate island in the Orkneys. In a wretched hut, he began assembling the female creature.

The work filled him with even greater revulsion than before. He worked at night, his hands trembling, his mind racing with terrible possibilities. What if the female creature refused to leave humanity alone? What if she was even more violent than her mate? What if they bred — creating a race of monsters that would plague the earth?

One evening, as he worked, Victor looked up and saw the creature watching him through the window. That face — yellow, grinning, expectant.

In that moment, Victor made his decision. He tore the half-finished female creature to pieces.

The creature let out a howl of fury and despair. "You dare break your promise? You dare destroy my only hope?"

"Begone!" Victor shouted. "I do break my promise. Never will I create another like yourself."

The creature's voice dropped to a whisper of terrible menace: "I shall be with you on your wedding-night."

Then he disappeared into the darkness.

6.title: Chapter 6: The Creature's Revenge
6.text:
Victor understood the threat but misinterpreted it. He believed the creature would come to kill him on his wedding night. He did not consider a more terrible possibility.

He disposed of the remains of the female creature by rowing out to sea and sinking them. Exhausted, he fell asleep in the boat and drifted to an Irish shore. There, he was immediately arrested — accused of murder.

The victim was Henry Clerval. Strangled. Victor recognized the marks on his friend's neck — the creature's enormous fingers. Victor collapsed into a months-long delirium, raving about the monster. He was eventually acquitted when evidence cleared him, and his father came to bring him home.

"Clerval, my friend, my benefactor—" Victor could barely speak of him. Henry Clerval, who had never harmed anyone, who had dreamed of poetry and adventure — destroyed because he was loved by Victor Frankenstein.

Victor returned to Geneva. Elizabeth was waiting, faithful and hopeful. He resolved to marry her — and to face whatever the creature had planned. He would arm himself. He would be ready.

They married at the end of summer. On their wedding evening, they traveled to an inn by Lake Geneva. As night fell, Victor armed himself and searched every passage and corner of the building. He told Elizabeth to retire to the bedroom while he kept watch.

A scream — terrible, piercing — came from the bedroom.

Victor burst through the door. Elizabeth lay across the bed, lifeless. The creature had strangled her. Through the open window, the monster stood in the moonlight, grinning and pointing at the body.

"I shall be with you on your wedding-night." He had meant Elizabeth.

Victor fired his pistol, but the creature plunged into the lake and escaped. Victor's father, upon hearing the news, suffered a stroke and died within days. Everything Victor had loved was gone — every person who had ever cared for him, destroyed by the thing he had created.

7.title: Chapter 7: The Pursuit
7.text:
With nothing left to lose, Victor swore an oath of vengeance. He would hunt the creature to the ends of the earth. He would destroy what he had made, or die trying.

He visited the graves of William, Elizabeth, and his father. Standing in the cemetery, he called out to the spirits of the dead and swore to pursue the murderer until one of them perished.

A laugh — low, fiendish — echoed through the darkness. The creature was there, listening.

"I am satisfied, miserable wretch!" the creature whispered. "You have determined to live, and I am satisfied."

So began a chase that spanned continents. The creature fled north — always north — leaving clues for Victor to follow. A mark on a tree, a message carved in stone: "My reign is not yet over. Follow me."

Victor pursued him through Russia, across frozen rivers, through howling blizzards. The creature always stayed ahead, seemingly superhuman in his endurance. Sometimes he left food for Victor — a cruel mockery of care, ensuring his pursuer survived long enough to keep suffering.

Local people occasionally reported seeing a gigantic figure passing through. Victor followed every lead, every rumor. He barely slept, barely ate. His health deteriorated, but his will never faltered.

The pursuit led to the Arctic. Victor obtained a dog-sled and drove across the ice. He was gaining — he could see the creature ahead. But the ice began to crack and break apart, and Victor found himself stranded on a shrinking fragment of frozen sea.

That was when Walton's ship found him — a man more dead than alive, consumed by a single purpose. Even aboard the ship, barely able to stand, Victor scanned the horizon for his creation.

"He is near," Victor told Walton. "I can feel it."

But his body was failing. The years of grief, guilt, and relentless pursuit had destroyed him.

8.title: Chapter 8: The Final Meeting
8.text:
Victor Frankenstein died aboard Walton's ship. His last words were a warning — and a plea.

"Seek happiness in tranquility and avoid ambition," he told Walton. "Yet why do I say this? I have myself been blasted in these hopes, yet another may succeed."

Even in death, he could not fully renounce the dream that had destroyed him. The quest for knowledge, for power over nature itself — it had been his ruin, but he could not call it entirely wrong. That was perhaps the most terrible thing of all.

Walton, shaken by Victor's story, made his decision. The crew had demanded they turn south — the ice was too dangerous. Walton agreed. The ambition that had driven him north was not worth the cost he had seen in Victor's eyes.

That night, Walton heard sounds from the cabin where Victor's body lay. He entered and found the creature, standing over his creator's corpse, weeping.

"That is also my victim!" the creature cried. "In his murder my crimes are consummated. Oh, Frankenstein! Generous and self-devoted being! What does it avail that I now ask thee to pardon me?"

Walton was struck by the depth of the creature's anguish. This was no mindless monster. This was a being of intelligence and feeling, consumed by loneliness and rage, who had wanted nothing more than to be accepted — and had destroyed everything in his path when that acceptance was denied.

"You hate me," the creature said to Walton, "but your abhorrence cannot equal that with which I regard myself. I, the miserable and the abandoned, am an abortion, to be spurned at, and kicked, and trampled on."

He spoke of his suffering — the beauty of the world he could see but never participate in, the love he felt for the De Lacey family, the hope that was crushed again and again. He had not been born evil. He had been made so by abandonment and cruelty.

"Once I falsely hoped to meet with beings who, pardoning my outward form, would love me for the excellent qualities which I was capable of unfolding," the creature said. "But now crime has degraded me beneath the meanest animal."

He declared his intention: he would travel to the northernmost extremity of the globe and build his own funeral pyre. He would burn, and the world would be rid of him. No one would ever know how to create another such being.

"I shall die," the creature said. "I shall no longer feel the agonies which now consume me. He is dead who called me into being; and when I shall be no more, the very remembrance of us both will speedily vanish."

He sprang from the cabin window onto an ice-raft that lay close to the vessel. He was soon borne away by the waves, and lost in darkness and distance.

Walton watched until the creature vanished into the Arctic night. Then he turned his ship south, toward home, carrying Victor's story — a warning for all who would seek to play God.

1.title: Chapter 1: The Narrator Arrives
1.text:
In my younger and more vulnerable years my father gave me some advice that I've been turning over in my mind ever since. "Whenever you feel like criticizing anyone," he told me, "just remember that all the people in this world haven't had the advantages that you've had."
