I am Nick Carraway. I grew up in the Middle West, in a prominent, well-to-do family. After graduating from Yale in 1915, I participated in the Great War, then moved to New York in the spring of 1922 to learn the bond business. I rented a small house in West Egg, on Long Island — one of the most extraordinary communities in North America.

My house was squeezed between two enormous mansions. The one to my right was a colossal affair — a factual imitation of some Hôtel de Ville in Normandy, with a marble swimming pool and more than forty acres of lawn and garden. It belonged to a man named Gatsby. I didn't know him yet.

Across the bay, the white palaces of fashionable East Egg glittered along the water. It was here that my cousin Daisy Buchanan lived with her husband Tom, whom I'd known in college. Tom was enormously wealthy — his family were enormously wealthy — and now he'd left Chicago and come East in a fashion that rather took your breath away.

I drove over to East Egg to have dinner with the Buchanans. Their house was even more elaborate than I expected — a cheerful red-and-white Georgian Colonial mansion overlooking the bay. Tom met me on the porch, his eyes arrogant and cruel.

Inside, I found Daisy and her friend Jordan Baker, a slender, small-breasted girl with an erect carriage. Daisy's face was sad and lovely with bright things in it — bright eyes and a bright passionate mouth. She told me she'd become "a beautiful little fool," and her voice was full of money.

During dinner, Tom received a phone call. Jordan told me it was Tom's woman in New York — everyone knew about her. Daisy's expression was tense and false.

That night, when I returned home, I noticed my neighbor Gatsby standing alone on his lawn. He stretched out his arms toward the dark water in a curious way, and I could have sworn he was trembling. I looked seaward — and distinguished nothing except a single green light, minute and far away, at the end of a dock.

2.title: Chapter 2: The Valley of Ashes
2.text:
About halfway between West Egg and New York the motor road hastily joins the railroad and runs beside it for a quarter of a mile, so as to shrink away from a certain desolate area of land. This is a valley of ashes — a fantastic farm where ashes grow like wheat into ridges and hills and grotesque gardens; where ashes take the forms of houses and chimneys.

Above the gray land and the spasms of bleak dust, the eyes of Doctor T. J. Eckleburg kept their vigil. The eyes are blue and gigantic — their retinas are one yard high. They look out of no face, but, instead, from a pair of enormous yellow spectacles which pass over a nonexistent nose. The billboard had been placed there by some wild oculist, but his eyes, dimmed a little by many paintless days under sun and rain, brood on over the solemn dumping ground.

Tom took me to meet his mistress. We stopped at Wilson's garage in the valley. George Wilson was a blond, spiritless man, anemic and faintly handsome. He was his wife's man and not his own. His wife Myrtle had a remarkable vitality about her — there was an immediately perceptible vitality about her person as if the nerves of her body were continually smouldering.

Tom, Myrtle, and I went to the apartment Tom kept in New York. Myrtle invited her sister Catherine and the McKees. The afternoon became a drunken party. Catherine told me that neither Tom nor Myrtle could stand the person they were married to.

Myrtle kept mentioning Daisy's name. "Daisy! Daisy! Daisy!" she shouted. "I'll say it whenever I want to! Daisy! Dai—"

Making a short deft movement, Tom Buchanan broke her nose with his open hand. There was a confusion, then quiet.

3.title: Chapter 3: Gatsby's Party
3.text:
There was music from my neighbor's house through the summer nights. In his blue gardens men and girls came and went like moths among the whisperings and the champagne and the stars. Every Friday five crates of oranges and lemons arrived from a fruiterer in New York — every Monday these same oranges and lemons left his back door in a pyramid of pulpless halves.

I was one of the few guests who had actually been invited. Most people came to Gatsby's parties without invitation. They conducted themselves according to the rules of behavior associated with an amusement park.

Jordan Baker and I wandered through the gardens, looking for our host. The bar was in full swing, and floating rounds of cocktails permeated the garden. Jordan and I found an owl-eyed man in Gatsby's library, amazed that the books were real.

"It's a triumph," the owl-eyed man declared. "What thoroughness! What realism! Knew when to stop, too — didn't cut the pages."

Finally, I met Gatsby himself. He had one of those rare smiles with a quality of eternal reassurance in it, that you may come across four or five times in life. It understood you just as far as you wanted to be understood, believed in you as you would like to believe in yourself.

"I'm Gatsby," he said. He was a year or two over thirty, and his elaborate formality of speech just missed being absurd. "I thought you ought to know," he said, smiling. "I'm afraid I'm not a very good host."

There was something gorgeous about him, some heightened sensitivity to the promises of life. It was an extraordinary gift for hope, a romantic readiness such as I have never found in any other person.

Late that night, Jordan told me an incredible thing about Gatsby — but she was interrupted, and the secret would wait.

4.title: Chapter 4: Gatsby's Past
4.text:
On Sunday morning while church bells rang in the villages along shore, Gatsby's car rolled up to my door. "Good morning, old sport," he said. "I'm going to make a big request of you today, so I thought you ought to know something about me."

He told me he was the son of wealthy people in the Middle West — San Francisco. "My family all died and I came into a good deal of money." His voice was solemn, as if the memory still haunted him. He'd been educated at Oxford — it was a family tradition.

I didn't believe a word of it. Then he produced a medal — "For Valour Extraordinary" — from Montenegro, and a photograph of himself at Oxford. I was skeptical, then I believed.

He asked me to invite Daisy to tea. "I want you to invite Daisy to your house some afternoon and then let me come over." The modesty of the demand shook me. He had waited five years for this moment.

Jordan told me the rest. In 1917, young Lieutenant Jay Gatsby had been stationed near Louisville, where he met Daisy Fay. She was eighteen, the most popular girl in town. Gatsby was penniless — a young man without a past and without a future. But when he kissed her, his mind would never romp again like the mind of God.

He went to war. Daisy waited, then didn't. She married Tom Buchanan of Chicago. On her wedding day, she received a letter from Gatsby and got drunk for the only time in her life, crying "Tell 'em all Daisy's changed her mine!"

But the next day, she married Tom. And Gatsby had spent the last five years buying that mansion across the bay, throwing those lavish parties — all for the single hope that Daisy might wander in one evening.

The green light at the end of Daisy's dock — that was what Gatsby had been reaching toward that night.

5.title: Chapter 5: The Reunion
5.text:
On the appointed day, it rained. Gatsby was pale with anxiety. He had sent a man to mow my lawn and had flowers delivered — my small house looked like a greenhouse.

When Daisy arrived, Gatsby was gone. Then I heard a knock at my front door — he had walked around the house and come in through the back. He was pale as death, with his hands plunged like weights in his coat pockets. He was trembling.

I left them alone for half an hour. When I came back, Daisy's face was smeared with tears, and Gatsby literally glowed — without a word or a gesture of exultation a new well-being radiated from him and filled the little room.

"It's stopped raining," Gatsby said. He smiled like — like an ecstatic patron of recurrent light. His count of enchanted objects had diminished by one. The green light that had symbolized his dream was now just an ordinary light on a dock again.

He took us to his mansion. Daisy admired everything — the gardens, the rooms, the swimming pool. He showed her his shirts, throwing them one by one before us, shirts of sheer linen and thick silk and fine flannel. Suddenly, Daisy bent her head into the shirts and began to cry stormily.

"They're such beautiful shirts," she sobbed. "It makes me sad because I've never seen such — such beautiful shirts before."

He had passed visibly through two states and was entering a third. The colossal significance of that green light had vanished forever. Compared to the great distance that had separated him from Daisy, the green light had seemed very near. Now it was again a green light on a dock. His count of enchanted objects had diminished by one.

Klipspringer played the piano. "In the morning, in the evening, ain't we got fun..." The afternoon slipped away, and they had forgotten me entirely.

6.title: Chapter 6: The Truth About Gatsby
6.text:
About this time an ambitious young reporter arrived one morning at Gatsby's door and asked him if he had anything to say. This was the first time I learned that Gatsby's notoriety had spread enough to make him news.

His real name was James Gatz of North Dakota. He had changed it at the age of seventeen — at the specific moment that witnessed the beginning of his career. He had been a clam-digger and a salmon-fisher, and his imagination had never really accepted his parents as his parents at all.

The truth was that Jay Gatsby of West Egg, Long Island, sprang from his Platonic conception of himself. He was a son of God — and he must be about His Father's business, the service of a vast, vulgar, and meretricious beauty.

At seventeen he met Dan Cody, a millionaire copper magnate, on Lake Superior. The old man took a liking to the boy and brought him aboard his yacht. For years Gatsby traveled with Cody — to the West Indies, the Barbary Coast, and on to wherever the yacht went. When Cody died, he left Gatsby twenty-five thousand dollars, but Cody's mistress prevented him from claiming it. Gatsby was left with his singularly appropriate education.

Tom Buchanan came to one of Gatsby's parties with Daisy. Tom was contemptuous. "Who is this Gatsby anyhow?" he demanded. "Some big bootlegger?" He sensed something between Gatsby and Daisy and didn't like it.

After everyone left, Gatsby told me he wanted to repeat the past. "Can't repeat the past?" he cried incredulously. "Why of course you can!"

He wanted nothing less than for Daisy to go to Tom and say: "I never loved you." Then they would go back to Louisville and be married from her house — just as if it were five years ago. He believed in the green light, the orgastic future that year by year recedes before us.

7.title: Chapter 7: The Confrontation
7.text:
It was the hottest day of the summer. Gatsby, Daisy, Tom, Jordan, and I drove to New York. The tension was unbearable. Tom had finally realized the truth about Gatsby and Daisy.

We ended up in a suite at the Plaza Hotel. Tom confronted Gatsby. "I found out what your 'drug-stores' were," he said. "You and this Wolfsheim bought up a lot of side-street drug-stores here and in Chicago and sold grain alcohol over the counter."

Gatsby denied nothing. Instead, he turned to Daisy: "Your wife doesn't love you. She's never loved you. She loves me."

"She does, though," Tom said. "The trouble is that sometimes she gets foolish ideas in her head and doesn't know what she's doing." He turned to Daisy: "Daisy, you loved me once — and you loved me too?"

Daisy hesitated. "I love you now — isn't that enough?" she said. "I can't help what's past." She began to cry. "Oh, you want too much!" she cried to Gatsby. "I love you now — isn't that enough? I did love him once — but I loved you too."

Gatsby's dream was crumbling. He wanted too much. He wanted Daisy to say she had never loved Tom, and she couldn't do that — because it wasn't true.

On the way home, Gatsby let Daisy drive his yellow car. In the valley of ashes, Myrtle Wilson ran into the road, thinking the car was Tom's. The car struck her and didn't stop. Myrtle was killed instantly.

Tom, driving behind in another car, found the scene. George Wilson was devastated, screaming. Tom told him the yellow car was Gatsby's — not his.

That night, I found Gatsby outside the Buchanans' house, watching over Daisy. He told me Daisy had been driving, but he would take the blame. "I want to wait here till Daisy goes to bed. Good night, old sport." He was watching over nothing.

8.title: Chapter 8: The End of the Dream
8.text:
I couldn't sleep all night. Toward dawn I heard a taxi go up Gatsby's drive and went over. His house had never seemed so enormous to me as it did that night.

"She'll call," Gatsby said. "She'll call tomorrow." He told me more about his past with Daisy — how he had kissed her in Louisville, how that kiss had forever changed him. "His life had been confused and disordered since then, but if he could once return to a certain starting place and go over it all slowly, he could find out what that thing was..."

I didn't want to leave him. "They're a rotten crowd," I shouted across the lawn. "You're worth the whole damn bunch put together." It was the only compliment I ever gave him.

At his office, I couldn't concentrate. Jordan called, but I didn't want to talk. I tried calling Gatsby but the line was busy.

George Wilson had been up all night. His neighbor found him muttering, "I have a way of finding out." He had seen the car. Tom had told him it was Gatsby's car. Wilson found his way to West Egg.

At two o'clock, Gatsby went for a swim — perhaps for the first time all summer. He must have looked up at an unfamiliar sky through frightening leaves and shivered. A new world, material without being real.

The chauffeur heard shots. Wilson had found Gatsby floating on an air mattress in the pool. He shot Gatsby, then turned the gun on himself.

I called Daisy immediately, but she and Tom had gone away — left no address, left no phone number. They were careless people, Tom and Daisy — they smashed up things and creatures and then retreated back into their money or their vast carelessness.

9.title: Chapter 9: The Aftermath
9.text:
Nobody came to Gatsby's funeral. I called everyone — Wolfsheim, the hundreds who had attended his parties, Klipspringer who had lived in his house. Nobody came. Daisy didn't send a message or a flower.

Gatsby's father, Henry C. Gatz, arrived from Minnesota. He was a solemn old man, very helpless and dismayed. He showed me a schedule young Jimmy had written in the back of a book: "Rise from bed... Practice elocution... Study needed inventions... Exercise... Study electricity..."

"He had a big future before him," the old man said. "If he'd of lived, he'd of been a great man."

The owl-eyed man was the only party guest who came. He stood in the rain by the grave.

Before I left, I walked over to Gatsby's mansion one last time. On the white steps an obscene word had been scrawled by some boy. I erased it.

I thought of Gatsby's wonder when he first picked out the green light at the end of Daisy's dock. He had come a long way to this blue lawn, and his dream must have seemed so close that he could hardly fail to grasp it. He did not know that it was already behind him.

Gatsby believed in the green light, the orgastic future that year by year recedes before us. It eluded us then, but that's no matter — tomorrow we will run faster, stretch out our arms farther... And one fine morning—

So we beat on, boats against the current, borne back ceaselessly into the past.

1.title: Chapter 1: A Single Man in Possession of a Good Fortune
1.text:
It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife. However little known the feelings or views of such a man may be on his first entering a neighbourhood, this truth is so well fixed in the minds of the surrounding families, that he is considered as the rightful property of some one or other of their daughters.

"My dear Mr. Bennet," said his lady to him one day, "have you heard that Netherfield Park is let at last?" Mr. Bennet replied that he had not. "But it is," returned she; "for Mrs. Long has just been here, and she told me all about it."

Mr. Bennet was so odd a mixture of quick parts, sarcastic humour, reserve, and caprice, that the experience of three-and-twenty years had been insufficient to make his wife understand his character. Her mind was less difficult to develop. She was a woman of mean understanding, little information, and uncertain temper. When she was discontented, she fancied herself nervous. The business of her life was to get her daughters married.

They had five daughters — Jane, the eldest and most beautiful; Elizabeth, the second, with fine eyes and a quick wit; Mary, the middle child, who sought accomplishment in books; Kitty, who followed wherever Lydia led; and Lydia, the youngest, wild and flirtatious at only fifteen.

"A single man of large fortune; four or five thousand a year. What a fine thing for our girls!" Mrs. Bennet exclaimed. She was already planning which daughter would catch his eye.

Mr. Bennet, beneath his dry wit, loved Elizabeth best. She had something more of quickness than her sisters.

2.title: Chapter 2: The Meryton Assembly
2.text:
Mr. Bingley was good-looking and gentlemanlike; he had a pleasant countenance, and easy, unaffected manners. His sisters were fine women, with an air of decided fashion. His friend Mr. Darcy soon drew the attention of the room by his fine, tall person, handsome features, noble mien, and the report which was in general circulation within five minutes after his entrance, of his having ten thousand a year.

The gentlemen pronounced him to be a fine figure of a man, the ladies declared he was much handsomer than Mr. Bingley. He was looked at with great admiration for about half the evening, till his manners gave a disgust which turned the tide of his popularity; for he was discovered to be proud — to be above his company, and above being pleased.

Mr. Bingley had danced with Jane twice. Elizabeth observed their interaction with warm approval — Jane's beauty and gentle manner had clearly captivated him. When she mentioned this to Charlotte Lucas, her friend agreed but cautioned: "If a woman conceals her affection with the same skill from the object of it, she may lose the opportunity of fixing him."

Darcy had declined to dance with anyone outside his own party. Bingley urged him: "I must have you dance. I hate to see you standing about by yourself in this stupid manner."

"I certainly shall not," said Darcy. "Your sisters are engaged, and there is not another woman in the room whom it would not be a punishment to me to stand up with."

"She is tolerable, but not handsome enough to tempt me," Darcy said of Elizabeth, loud enough for her to overhear. Elizabeth told the story with great spirit among her friends, for she had a lively, playful disposition which delighted in anything ridiculous.

3.title: Chapter 3: Growing Acquaintance
3.text:
The ladies of Longbourn soon waited on those of Netherfield. Miss Bingley and Mrs. Hurst found Jane delightful and returned her visit. Their regard for Elizabeth was less — they considered her beneath their notice.

At a gathering at Lucas Lodge, Darcy found himself watching Elizabeth. There was something in her dark eyes — a mixture of sweetness and archness — that began to attract him despite himself. He had at first scarcely allowed her to be pretty; but no sooner had he made it clear to himself and his friends that she had hardly a good feature in her face, than he began to find it was rendered uncommonly intelligent by the beautiful expression of her dark eyes.

Elizabeth could not fathom why Mr. Darcy was staring at her. She mentioned it to Charlotte: "He has a very satirical eye, and if I do not begin by being impertinent myself, I shall soon grow afraid of him."

Sir William Lucas attempted to pair them for a dance. "My dear Miss Eliza, why are you not dancing? Mr. Darcy, you must allow me to present this young lady to you as a very desirable partner."

Darcy was not unwilling, but Elizabeth drew back. "Indeed, sir, I have not the least intention of dancing. Do not suppose that I moved this way in order to beg for a partner."

Miss Bingley, perceiving Darcy's growing interest, warned him: "I can guess the subject of your reverie. You are considering how insupportable it would be to spend many evenings in such society."

"I have been meditating on the very great pleasure which a pair of fine eyes in the face of a pretty woman can bestow," Darcy replied. When pressed for the lady's name, he said calmly: "Miss Elizabeth Bennet."

Miss Bingley was astonished and not a little jealous.

4.title: Chapter 4: Jane Falls Ill at Netherfield
4.text:
Jane received an invitation to dine at Netherfield with Miss Bingley and Mrs. Hurst. Mrs. Bennet, scheming, refused to let her take the carriage — knowing it would rain and Jane would be forced to stay overnight.

The plan worked too well. Jane was caught in the rain and fell seriously ill. Elizabeth, deeply concerned, walked three miles through muddy fields to reach Netherfield. She arrived with weary ankles, dirty stockings, and a face glowing with the warmth of exercise.

Miss Bingley and Mrs. Hurst were shocked by her appearance. But Darcy was struck — her eyes were brightened by the exercise. Elizabeth attended to Jane with devoted care.

During her stay at Netherfield, Elizabeth's conversations with Darcy grew more spirited. She challenged his pride; he was intrigued by her refusal to flatter him.

"Do you prefer reading to cards?" Darcy asked one evening. "That is rather singular."

"Miss Eliza Bennet," said Miss Bingley, "despises cards. She is a great reader, and has no pleasure in anything else."

"I deserve neither such praise nor such censure," cried Elizabeth; "I am not a great reader, and I have pleasure in many things."

Darcy observed her silently. Miss Bingley, exasperated, tried every art to draw his attention — walking about the room, complimenting him — but Darcy's eyes kept returning to Elizabeth.

"Your pride," Elizabeth said to him directly, "your defects — what are they?"

"My good opinion, once lost, is lost forever," Darcy admitted.

"That is a failing indeed!" cried Elizabeth. "Implacable resentment is a shade in a character. But you have chosen your fault well. I really cannot laugh at it."

When Jane recovered, the Bennet sisters returned home. Darcy was relieved — and troubled to find how much he had noticed her absence.

5.title: Chapter 5: Mr. Collins and Mr. Wickham
5.text:
A new character entered the scene: Mr. Collins, a clergyman and the Bennet estate's heir. He was a tall, heavy-looking young man of five-and-twenty. His air was grave and stately, and his manners were very formal. He had come to Longbourn with the intention of selecting a wife from among the Bennet daughters.

Mr. Collins was a mixture of servility and self-importance. He spoke at great length about his patroness, Lady Catherine de Bourgh — her estate at Rosings Park, her condescension, her fireplace. Everything he said returned, inevitably, to Lady Catherine.

"She is perfectly amiable," Mr. Collins declared, "and has even condescended to advise me to marry. 'Mr. Collins,' she said, 'you must marry. Choose properly, choose a gentlewoman for my sake.'"

His eye first fell on Jane, but Mrs. Bennet steered him toward Elizabeth instead — Jane, she hinted, was likely to be soon engaged. Collins readily transferred his affections.

Meanwhile, the militia had arrived in Meryton, and with them came Mr. Wickham — an officer whose countenance was greatly in his favour. He was blessed with such happy manners as may ensure his making friends, and his appearance was remarkably agreeable.

Elizabeth was drawn to Wickham immediately. He was charming, open, and attentive. During a walk, Wickham told Elizabeth a remarkable story: he and Darcy had grown up together. Darcy's father, old Mr. Darcy, had been Wickham's godfather and had promised him a valuable church living.

"But Mr. Darcy chose to disregard his father's wishes," Wickham said, his handsome face clouded with sorrow. "He gave the living to another man. I was left with nothing."

Elizabeth was outraged. This confirmed everything she had felt about Darcy's pride and cruelty. Wickham was the injured party — gentle, wronged, and brave.

6.title: Chapter 6: The Netherfield Ball and Mr. Collins's Proposal
6.text:
The ball at Netherfield was the event of the season. Elizabeth had promised the first two dances to Mr. Collins — a decision she deeply regretted. Collins danced with more enthusiasm than skill, frequently moving the wrong way, and apologizing with such elaborate formality that Elizabeth wished the earth would swallow them both.

When she danced with Darcy, the conversation was electric with tension. She brought up Wickham deliberately. Darcy's expression darkened. "Mr. Wickham is blessed with such happy manners as may ensure his making friends — whether he may be equally capable of retaining them, is less certain."

"He has been unlucky in losing your friendship," said Elizabeth with feeling, "and in a manner which he is likely to suffer from all his life."

Darcy was silent, and they parted with mutual irritation.

At supper, Mrs. Bennet spoke loudly of Jane and Bingley's expected engagement, mortifying Elizabeth and amusing no one. Mary sang badly. Mr. Collins gave a pompous speech about the pleasure of music. Elizabeth burned with shame for her family.

The next morning brought a worse trial. Mr. Collins proposed to Elizabeth. He approached it like a business presentation, listing his reasons: Lady Catherine's approval, his comfortable living, the entailment of the estate, and Mrs. Bennet's encouragement.

"Accept him!" Mrs. Bennet cried, "or I will never see you again!"

"An unhappy alternative is before you, Elizabeth," said Mr. Bennet, his eyes twinkling. "From this day you must be a stranger to one of your parents. Your mother will never see you again if you do not marry Mr. Collins, and I will never see you again if you do."

Collins, barely wounded, promptly proposed to Charlotte Lucas — and Charlotte accepted. Elizabeth was stunned. Charlotte, sensible Charlotte, marrying that ridiculous man? But Charlotte was twenty-seven, plain, and practical. "I am not romantic, you know," Charlotte told her. "I ask only a comfortable home."

Shortly after, Bingley departed Netherfield without explanation. Miss Bingley wrote to Jane that they were all settled in London and would not return. Jane was heartbroken but hid it behind her gentle composure. Elizabeth was furious — she was certain Darcy and Bingley's sisters had separated them.

7.title: Chapter 7: Darcy's Letter
7.text:
Elizabeth visited Charlotte at Hunsford, near Rosings Park. There she encountered Darcy again — he was visiting his aunt, Lady Catherine de Bourgh. Lady Catherine was exactly as Mr. Collins had described: imperious, condescending, and utterly convinced of her own importance.

Darcy called at the parsonage frequently. His behavior confused Elizabeth — he seemed to seek her company, but said little. He stared at her with an intensity she could not read.

Then, one evening, he appeared at the parsonage alone. He was agitated, pacing the room. Elizabeth watched him in astonishment.

"In vain I have struggled. It will not do. My feelings will not be repressed. You must allow me to tell you how ardently I admire and love you."

Elizabeth's astonishment was beyond expression. She stared, coloured, doubted, and was silent. He spoke well, but his proposal was as much about the obstacles — her family's inferiority, the degradation of connecting himself with the Bennets — as it was about love.

She refused him. "I might wonder why, with so evident a desire of offending and insulting me, you chose to tell me that you liked me against your will, against your reason, and even against your character."

He was shocked. She pressed further — Wickham's story, Jane and Bingley. "You have reduced him to his present state of poverty. You have done all this! And yet you can treat the mention of his misfortunes with contempt and ridicule."

"And this," cried Darcy, walking with quick steps across the room, "is your opinion of me! Perhaps these offences might have been overlooked, had not your pride been hurt by my honest confession."

They parted in mutual anger.

The next morning, Darcy delivered a letter. In it, he laid bare the truth. Wickham was not the injured party — he was a liar and a scoundrel who had attempted to elope with Darcy's fifteen-year-old sister, Georgiana, for her fortune. As for Jane and Bingley — Darcy had intervened because he genuinely believed Jane did not return Bingley's feelings.

Elizabeth read the letter once, then again, then again. Gradually, painfully, she recognized the truth. She had been blind — prejudiced against Darcy, charmed by Wickham's lies. "Till this moment I never knew myself," she whispered.

8.title: Chapter 8: Reunited at Pemberley
8.text:
That summer, Elizabeth toured Derbyshire with her aunt and uncle, the Gardiners. They visited Pemberley, Darcy's estate, believing him to be away. The house was beautiful — large and handsome, standing well on rising ground, backed by a ridge of high woody hills. Elizabeth felt something she had not expected: admiration, and the strange thought that she might have been mistress of all this.

The housekeeper, Mrs. Reynolds, spoke of Darcy with glowing warmth. "He is the best landlord and the best master that ever lived. I have never had a cross word from him in my life." She showed them his portrait — and Elizabeth stood before it, looking at the handsome face with a deeper interest than she had ever felt.

Then Darcy himself appeared. He was changed. His manners were gentle, warm, almost humble. He introduced himself to the Gardiners with courtesy. He asked Elizabeth to meet his sister Georgiana — a shy, sweet girl, nothing like the proud creature Wickham had described.

Elizabeth was bewildered. Could this be the same proud man? Had her refusal truly changed him?

Their fragile new understanding was shattered by terrible news: Lydia had eloped with Wickham. They were not married — Lydia had run away with a man who had no intention of marrying her. The scandal would ruin the entire Bennet family.

Elizabeth told Darcy, expecting his contempt. Instead, he listened gravely and departed. She was certain she had lost him forever.

But she had not. Unknown to Elizabeth, Darcy tracked down Wickham in London. He paid Wickham's considerable debts and essentially bribed him to marry Lydia — all in secret, asking nothing in return. When Elizabeth learned the truth from her aunt's letter, she was overwhelmed.

Bingley returned to Netherfield — clearly at Darcy's encouragement — and proposed to Jane. The family's joy was unbounded.

Then Darcy came again to Longbourn. Walking with Elizabeth, he spoke with none of the pride that had marked his first proposal. "You are too generous to trifle with me. If your feelings are still what they were last April, tell me so at once. My affections and wishes are unchanged, but one word from you will silence me on this subject forever."

"My feelings are so different," Elizabeth said, smiling through tears, "that I now receive your assurances with gratitude and pleasure."

They walked on, not knowing where they went. They talked of how their feelings had changed — his pride humbled, her prejudice dissolved. Lady Catherine's furious visit, meant to prevent the match, had actually given Darcy hope: if Elizabeth had truly been indifferent, she would have simply said so.

"I cannot fix on the hour, or the spot, or the look, or the words, which laid the foundation," Darcy told her. "It is too long ago. I was in the middle before I knew that I had begun."

Mr. Bennet, when asked for his consent, was astounded. "Are you out of your senses? Have not you always hated him?" But when he saw Elizabeth's happiness, he gave his blessing.

And so Elizabeth Bennet became Elizabeth Darcy — not because she wanted Pemberley, not because she needed security, but because she had found in the proudest man she knew a heart capable of true change, and in herself, the humility to recognize it.

1.title: Chapter 1: The Feud
1.text:
The city of Verona lay baking in the summer heat, its cobblestone streets winding between tall stone houses whose shutters were thrown open to catch whatever breeze might drift up from the river. It was a city of beauty and of blood — where merchants sold silks beside butchers' stalls, and where ancient hatreds coiled beneath the surface of every civil greeting.

The trouble began, as it so often did, with servants. Two men wearing the livery of the House of Capulet swaggered into the marketplace, their thumbs hooked in their belts and their chins thrust forward with the particular arrogance of men who borrow their courage from their masters' name. They spotted two servants of the Montague household crossing the square and made no effort to step aside.

Words were exchanged — small, poisonous words, the kind that cost nothing to speak and everything to answer. A thumb bitten in contempt. A challenge issued. Within moments, blades were drawn, and the quiet morning erupted into chaos. Shopkeepers dove behind their counters; women snatched children off the streets; pigeons burst skyward from the eaves in a great beating of wings.

Benvolio, nephew to Lord Montague, arrived to find his kinsmen already bleeding. He drew his sword not to fight but to part them — "Part, fools! Put up your swords; you know not what you do!" — but Tybalt of the Capulet line came striding through the crowd like a thundercloud given human form. He was a young man built for war, with a fighter's narrow eyes and a temper like a struck flint.

"What, drawn and talk of peace? I hate the word, as I hate hell, all Montagues, and thee," Tybalt snarled, and lunged.

The brawl spread through the marketplace like fire through dry grass. Citizens who had lived their whole lives in the shadow of this feud took sides or fled. The clash of steel rang off the stone facades until the sound of trumpets cut through everything — the Prince's guard, armored and mounted, forcing their horses between the fighting men.

Escalus, Prince of Verona, surveyed the wreckage with cold fury. He had seen this scene too many times: blood on the stones, torn clothing, men holding wounds, the same two names on every pair of lips. He raised his voice until it filled the square.

"Three civil brawls, bred of an airy word by thee, old Capulet, and Montague, have thrice disturbed the quiet of our streets." His gaze moved between the two patriarchs who had now arrived, both white-haired men whose hands still knew how to grip a sword. "If ever you disturb our streets again, your lives shall pay the forfeit of the peace."

The crowd dispersed. The square emptied of all but the injured and the shamed. Lord Montague watched his nephew Benvolio with worried eyes and asked after his son.

Romeo had not been in the brawl. He had been seen that morning walking in the sycamore grove west of the city, before the sun was up — pacing alone among the trees with his cloak wrapped tight despite the warmth, his eyes red from a sleepless night. He had slipped away when he heard anyone approaching.

Benvolio found him later, sitting on a low wall and staring at nothing. Romeo was seventeen, with the kind of face that had not yet decided whether it was handsome, and eyes that were too old for his age. He had been in love — or thought himself in love — with a girl named Rosaline for the better part of the summer. She was a Capulet, fair and cold and utterly indifferent to him, and he had transformed this indifference into the grandest tragedy he could imagine.

"In sadness, cousin, I do love a woman," Romeo confessed, as if delivering a funeral oration. He spoke of Rosaline's beauty with the fervor of a man composing his own epitaph, rhyming and sighing until Benvolio wanted to shake him by the shoulders. She had sworn herself to chastity. She did not want him. She had barely looked at him.

"Be ruled by me," Benvolio said, with the patience of someone who had listened to this for weeks. "Forget to think of her."

"O, teach me how I should forget to think," Romeo said, gazing at the horizon.

Benvolio did not have an answer for that. The city stretched around them in the golden afternoon, and somewhere beneath its beauty, two families sharpened their hatred like a knife, and waited.

2.title: Chapter 2: The Masquerade
2.text:
Lord Capulet was in fine spirits. His house had been full of noise and preparation for a week, servants rushing from room to room with armloads of flowers and candles, the kitchens roaring with ovens from dawn to midnight. He was throwing a feast — a proper Capulet feast, the kind meant to remind all of Verona where beauty and wealth resided.

He had also, almost as an afterthought, begun to consider the question of his daughter's future. Paris was a fine young nobleman, a kinsman to the Prince himself, with steady eyes and a decent fortune. He had come to Capulet asking for Juliet's hand in marriage, and Capulet had told him to woo her, to win her heart — she was not yet fourteen, and he was not a man who wished to rush such things. "The earth hath swallowed all my hopes but she," he had said, thinking of the children he had buried. "She is the hopeful lady of my earth."

A Capulet servant, tasked with delivering invitations and unable to read, stopped two young men in the street and asked them to help him puzzle out the names on the list. Those two young men were Romeo and Benvolio. Benvolio read the list aloud — and there, among the guests invited to Capulet's house that very night, was the name of Rosaline.

"Go thither," Benvolio said, his eyes lighting up, "and with unattainted eye compare her face with some that I shall show, and I will make thee think thy swan a crow." He meant to cure Romeo of this obsession the only way he could imagine: by showing him that other beautiful women existed in the world.

Romeo agreed, as a man agrees to anything when he is bored enough with his own suffering. They would go masked — it was a feast, and masks were expected, and the Capulets could not be expected to know every face in Verona. Their friend Mercutio came with them, quick-tongued and restless, filling the walk to the Capulet house with a long, dazzling speech about a fairy called Queen Mab who visited sleeping men and gave them dreams.

The house blazed with light when they arrived. Music poured from the windows. Masked guests moved through rooms hung with garlands, and servants carried trays of wine, and the whole evening smelled of roses and candle-wax and expensive perfume. Romeo moved through it in a kind of daze, scanning faces for Rosaline, only half-listening to the music.

Then he saw her.

She was standing across the room, talking to her nurse — a girl in a pale dress, with dark hair and eyes that caught the candlelight. Romeo stopped walking. Some essential gear in the machinery of his heart caught and turned.

"What lady is that which doth enrich the hand of yonder knight?" he asked a passing servant, his voice barely above a whisper.

The servant did not know. Romeo watched her move through the room and felt the whole elaborate architecture of his devotion to Rosaline collapse like paper in rain. He had not known what love was until this moment. "Did my heart love till now? Forswear it, sight, for I ne'er saw true beauty till this night."

He was not the only one watching.

Tybalt had recognized Romeo's voice — the Montague voice, unmistakable even under a mask, even in a room full of laughter. He went to his uncle, red with fury, demanding permission to throw the Montague out, to cut him down if necessary. Lord Capulet, in the magnanimous mood of a man whose party is going well, refused. Romeo had done nothing. He was behaving himself. To cause a scene now would be worse than tolerating the intrusion.

Tybalt withdrew, barely controlled, his hand already resting on the hilt of his sword. He would remember this.

Romeo found his way to the girl. He took her hand and spoke to her — she was quick and sharp and not at all overwhelmed, meeting his poetry with her own, giving as much as she took. Her name was Juliet. She laughed at him a little, and he did not mind at all.

The Nurse appeared at her elbow. Juliet was called away to her mother. Romeo watched her go and turned to the Nurse with the question already on his lips.

"Her mother is the lady of the house," the Nurse said.

The room seemed to tilt. A Capulet. He had tumbled into love with a Capulet. "My life is my foe's debt," he said quietly, and the words had the weight of something already decided.

Juliet, on her way to her mother, pulled the Nurse aside. "What's he that follows there, that would not dance?"

The Nurse told her.

Juliet stood very still for a moment. "My only love sprung from my only hate," she said, and could not explain to anyone, least of all herself, why it felt like fate rather than misfortune.

3.title: Chapter 3: The Balcony
3.text:
Romeo could not leave. His friends had gone — he had heard Mercutio calling for him, the familiar voice receding into the dark street beyond the orchard wall, joking that Romeo must have gone home to bed. He had not gone home to bed. He had climbed the orchard wall and dropped down into the Capulet garden, and now he stood among the trees with the night air on his face, looking up at a lit window.

He knew it was madness. He knew what he was risking. He stood there anyway, hidden among the trees, watching the light from her window fall golden onto the grass.

Then the window opened, and Juliet came out onto the small balcony above, and Romeo forgot to breathe.

She did not see him. She stood at the balcony rail with the night wrapped around her like a garment, and she spoke into the air — not knowing she had an audience, not knowing that every word fell on a pair of ears that would hold them forever.

"O Romeo, Romeo — wherefore art thou Romeo?" She meant it not as a question of location but of identity — why must you be a Romeo, a Montague, the one name that stands between us and everything? "Deny thy father and refuse thy name, or if thou wilt not, be but sworn my love, and I'll no longer be a Capulet."

He could not keep silent. He stepped out from under the trees. "Shall I hear more, or shall I speak at this?"

She gasped. She leaned over the railing, trying to see him in the dark. "What man art thou that, thus bescreened in night, so stumblest on my counsel?"

"By a name I know not how to tell thee who I am," he said. "My name, dear saint, is hateful to myself, because it is an enemy to thee."

She recognized his voice. A dozen emotions crossed her face in quick succession — alarm and wonder and something she was already trying to suppress because it was too large to look at directly. "How camest thou hither, tell me, and wherefore? The orchard walls are high and hard to climb, and the place death, considering who thou art, if any of my kinsmen find thee here."

"With love's light wings did I o'erperch these walls," he told her, "for stony limits cannot hold love out."

There was a pause. She gripped the railing. "Dost thou love me? I know thou wilt say 'ay,' and I will take thy word; yet, if thou swear'st, thou mayst prove false." She was trying to be careful. She was seventeen years old and she had met him two hours ago at a party and she was trying, earnestly and unsuccessfully, to be careful. "O gentle Romeo, if thou dost love, pronounce it faithfully."

"Lady, by yonder blessed moon I swear—"

"O, swear not by the moon," she interrupted, "the inconstant moon, that monthly changes in her circle orb, lest that thy love prove likewise variable."

"What shall I swear by?"

"Do not swear at all," she said. "Or if thou wilt, swear by thy gracious self, which is the god of my idolatry, and I'll believe thee."

They stood in the dark and the garden, and between them was something that had no name yet but was quickly becoming one. The Nurse called from inside the house — Juliet's name floating out through the window — and Juliet turned and then turned back, unable to end it.

"If that thy bent of love be honorable, thy purpose marriage," she said, all the caution in her voice replaced now by something steadier and more serious, "send me word tomorrow, by one that I'll procure to come to thee, where and what time thou wilt perform the rite, and all my fortunes at thy foot I'll lay, and follow thee my lord throughout the world."

The Nurse called again. Juliet went inside and came back and went inside again, each departure and return like a tide. "Good night, good night! Parting is such sweet sorrow that I shall say good night till it be morrow."

Romeo stood in the empty garden for a long time after the light in her window went out. What's in a name? he thought. A rose by any other name would smell as sweet — and a Capulet, loved enough, might as well be a Montague. He had one plan now, and it was simple: marry her. He would go to Friar Lawrence at first light, and he would begin.

4.title: Chapter 4: The Secret Wedding
4.text:
Friar Lawrence was in his garden when Romeo arrived, and the old man had been awake since before dawn, gathering herbs in the grey morning light. He moved among his plants with the careful attention of someone who understood that the same root could heal or kill depending on how you used it. He was a practical man — gentle, learned, given to long speeches — and he had known Romeo since the boy was small enough to sit on his knee.

He listened to Romeo with a mixture of amusement and concern. Not twelve hours earlier, Romeo had been in this same garden weeping over Rosaline. Now he stood before the friar with shining eyes, talking about a girl named Juliet Capulet as though she had been woven from starlight. The whiplash of it would have been comical if the circumstances were not so dangerous.

"Holy Saint Francis, what a change is here! Is Rosaline, that thou didst love so dear, so soon forsaken?" the Friar asked, setting down his basket. "Young men's love then lies not truly in their hearts, but in their eyes."

"Thou chidest me oft for loving Rosaline," Romeo said, untroubled by the comparison.

"For doting, not for loving, pupil mine." The Friar studied him. This was not the same boy who had been sighing in the grove yesterday. This was something else — steadier, more serious. "But come, young waverer, come, go with me, in one respect I'll thy assistant be."

He had a reason beyond mere sentiment. A marriage between a Montague and a Capulet — quietly done, secretly done — might be exactly the knot that tied the two families' hatred into something that could unravel it. It was the kind of calculation that a man of God permitted himself when he believed the outcome was good enough to justify the means. "For this alliance may so happy prove to turn your households' rancor to pure love."

The Nurse was Juliet's messenger. She was a broad, comfortable woman who had been with Juliet since birth, who had nursed her and sung to her and scolded her and loved her with the fierce uncomplicated love of someone who has no children of her own. She appeared at Romeo's elbow in the street that morning, puffing from the walk, and subjected him to a long examination before she was satisfied. She wanted assurances. She wanted to know he was serious, that he would not use Juliet and leave her to grieve.

Romeo gave her every assurance. He was earnest and young and completely in earnest, and the Nurse, who had hoped for Paris and his steady fortune, looked at this Montague boy and saw something in his face that made her decide to trust him. She went back to Juliet with the message: come to Friar Lawrence's cell this afternoon, under the pretense of going to confession, and there you will be married.

Juliet received the news in her bedroom with her whole body — she could not stand still, pacing from window to door and back, her hands clasped tight. She had known him for one day. She was going to marry him.

"Hie to high fortune!" she said, when the Nurse had finished. "Honest Nurse, farewell."

They met in the small, spare room behind the chapel, where the light came through a single high window and lay in a narrow bar across the stone floor. Romeo was already there when Juliet arrived, and for a moment they both simply looked at each other — the reality of the person standing in daylight, so much more solid and present than a voice in a dark garden.

Friar Lawrence joined their hands and spoke the words. He was not without misgiving — he spoke of loving moderately, of not burning too bright, of the way a violent delight tends toward a violent end. But the two before him were not listening to his cautions; they were looking at each other with the particular blind faith of people who believe the universe has been arranged for their benefit.

It was done. They were married. The whole thing had taken less than a day from first glance to wedding, and Verona went about its ordinary business outside the chapel walls, entirely unaware.

5.title: Chapter 5: Blood in the Streets
5.text:
Mercutio was dying in the street, and he was furious about it.

The day had been too hot for anyone's patience. The city shimmered with a heat that turned tempers brittle, and Mercutio and Benvolio had been walking through the streets in the late afternoon when Tybalt found them. Tybalt had not forgotten the feast. He had filed the insult of Romeo's uninvited presence away and sharpened it for a week, and now he walked toward them with a group of Capulet men and the controlled precision of someone who has decided what he is going to do.

He wanted Romeo. He had sent a letter to the Montague house already, a formal challenge. But Romeo was not here, and Mercutio was — Mercutio with his sharp tongue and his quicker blade, who had no patience for the elaborate courtesies of formal dueling, who called Tybalt's careful footwork "a fantasy, a trick" and goaded him with a ferocious, laughing contempt.

Romeo appeared just as the exchange of insults was reaching the point of no return. He came down the street still glowing from the morning's ceremony, still carrying the secret of his marriage like a lantern inside his chest, and when Tybalt turned to him with the challenge ready on his lips, Romeo felt nothing but goodwill.

"Tybalt, the reason that I have to love thee doth much excuse the appertaining rage to such a greeting," Romeo said. He was trying to tell the man that they were family now, that the feud was over for him, that he meant it. "I do protest I never injured thee, but love thee better than thou canst devise."

Tybalt heard only cowardice.

Mercutio heard the same thing, and it enraged him. He drew his sword on Romeo's behalf, on behalf of the Montague honor that Romeo was apparently too lovesick to defend. "O calm, dishonorable, vile submission! Tybalt, you rat-catcher, will you walk?"

What happened next happened too fast for intention. Romeo tried to get between them — grabbed Mercutio's arm, put himself in the middle — and in the confusion of bodies Tybalt's blade found a gap. Not deep. Not a killing wound, at first glance. Mercutio laughed it off: "Ay, ay, a scratch, a scratch — marry, 'tis enough."

But the light was going out of his eyes even as he said it, and when Benvolio reached for him, he felt the warm weight of a man who is losing something he cannot get back.

"A plague on both your houses!" Mercutio said. He said it three times, each time with less breath behind it, and the third time it came out barely above a whisper. He died in Benvolio's arms on the hot stone street.

Romeo stood over his friend's body and felt the glow inside him go cold. Everything he had decided that morning — peace, family, love — burned away. He looked at Tybalt, who had returned with the swagger of a man who expects no consequences, and Romeo picked up his sword.

"Tybalt, Mercutio's soul is but a little way above our heads," he said, very quietly. "Either thou, or I, or both, must go with him."

The fight was brief and total. Romeo killed Tybalt with the concentrated fury of grief, a single clean fact at the end of a day that had been full of impossible things. Benvolio was already pulling at his arm before the body stopped moving — citizens were gathering, the Prince's men would come, he had to run.

Romeo ran.

He had time, in the running, to understand what he had done. Juliet's cousin. Her blood, now spilled by his hand. Tybalt, who was now his kinsman by marriage and whom he had just killed in the street.

Prince Escalus pronounced sentence that evening: Romeo Montague was banished from Verona. To return was to die. It was mercy, some said — the Prince had shown mercy. Romeo, sitting in Friar Lawrence's cell with his face in his hands, did not feel the mercy of it.

"Ha, banishment? Be merciful, say 'death,'" he said. "For exile hath more terror in his look than death."

He meant it entirely.

6.title: Chapter 6: The Separation
6.text:
Juliet learned what had happened from the Nurse, who told it badly — weeping and wringing her hands, speaking of blood and death without first making clear whose blood and whose death, so that for several terrible minutes Juliet believed Romeo was dead. When the truth sorted itself out — Tybalt dead, Romeo banished — she sat with the two facts and tried to hold them both at once.

Tybalt was her cousin. She had grown up with him, had seen him at every feast and family gathering, had been the recipient of his gruff and somewhat frightening affection. He was dead. Romeo had killed him.

"Shall I speak ill of him that is my husband?" she said to herself, alone in her room, working through it like someone pressing on a bruise to map its exact dimensions. She could not. Romeo had been provoked. Tybalt would have killed Romeo. The fact of the marriage — real and binding, witnessed by God and a friar — meant she was Romeo's before she was a Capulet. Grief for Tybalt was real, and it sat beside her love for Romeo without destroying it, and she held both and breathed.

What she could not hold was the word banished.

Romeo came to her that night, guided by the Nurse, slipping through the city in the dark with his execution hanging over any ill-timed encounter. He came to her window and she pulled him inside, and they had the night — one night, their first and as it would turn out their last as ordinary husband and wife in an ordinary room, with the city sleeping around them.

They lay in the dark and listened to each other breathe and did not speak of what was coming. When the birds began before dawn, they argued about it — Romeo said it was the lark, the morning bird, time to go; Juliet said no, it was the nightingale, and time was not yet. They both knew she was wrong.

"It was the lark, the herald of the morn," Romeo said finally. "I must be gone and live, or stay and die."

Juliet let him go. She stood at the window and watched him descend and felt, with a certainty she could not explain, that she was watching something more than his departure. He looked up at her from below, small in the grey light. She thought: why do you look so pale? He thought: she looks as one dead in the bottom of a tomb.

Neither of them said it. He went.

The Capulet house had no time to absorb one grief before another arrived. Lord Capulet, believing that Juliet's sorrow was for Tybalt and that motion would cure her, had accelerated his plans with Paris. The wedding, previously discussed in vague terms of someday, was now set for Thursday — three days hence. It was Monday.

Juliet refused.

Lord Capulet had not heard no from his daughter before. He stood in her room with his face reddening through every shade from pink to scarlet and delivered himself of an ultimatum with such velocity and volume that the Nurse and Lady Capulet both took a step back. He called her ungrateful, disobedient, a curse upon him. He told her she would go to the church on Thursday or she would go to the street — he would drag her there himself if necessary.

"I'll not wed," Juliet said, her voice very steady. "I cannot love. I do beseech you, give me strength."

Her father left, slamming every door between her room and the courtyard. Her mother followed without a word, unable or unwilling to intercede. The Nurse, when Juliet turned to her, offered the counsel of a practical woman who had watched the world longer: Romeo was as good as dead to her now, banished and unreachable, and Paris was a good man, better-looking than Romeo in the Nurse's honest assessment. She should marry Paris. What difference did it make?

Juliet looked at her nurse for a long moment. Then she said she would go to Friar Lawrence for confession, to be forgiven for having made her father angry. She said it very calmly.

She did not mention that she intended to die before she married Paris.

7.title: Chapter 7: The Friar's Plan
7.text:
Friar Lawrence looked at the girl standing in his cell and understood that she was serious. She had a knife in her hand — not raised, not threatening, just present — and the expression on her face was the expression of someone who has already made the necessary calculations and arrived at a settled answer. If he had no solution, she would find her own.

He had a solution. He was not certain it was a good one, but it was the only one he had.

"Hold, daughter," he said, and told her about the vial.

He had distilled it himself — a compound of herbs, the kind of knowledge monks accumulated over long years of study, hovering in the borderland between medicine and something older. Drink it, and within minutes every sign of life would vanish: no pulse, no breath, no color. She would appear dead. The effect would last forty-two hours, after which it would release her as cleanly as sleep releasing the sleeper at dawn.

They would put her in the Capulet tomb, as they did with all the family dead, in the great vault beneath the church. Romeo would be told — the Friar would send a letter to Mantua, where Romeo had gone into exile, explaining everything. Romeo would come to the tomb and be there when she woke. They would slip away together to Mantua, and some future pardon from the Prince would allow them to come home.

Juliet took the vial. She did not ask many questions. Either the plan worked or she was dead in a tomb, and both outcomes seemed preferable to marrying Paris while Romeo lived and breathed forty miles away in Mantua.

She went home. She found her father and apologized — told him she had been willful and foolish, told him she would marry Paris. Lord Capulet, relieved and softened, moved the wedding up a day to Wednesday. It did not occur to him to wonder at the speed of her conversion.

That night, Juliet sent the Nurse away and sat alone with the vial for a long time. She was afraid. Not of death — or not only of death — but of what lay between. What if the potion did not work, and she woke up married to Paris? What if it worked too well and never released her? What if she woke in the tomb before Romeo came, in the dark, among the bones of her ancestors, with the smell of stone and old death all around her? What if she went mad?

She thought of Romeo. She drank.

Friar Lawrence's letter never reached Romeo.

The Friar had sent it by Brother John, who traveled toward Mantua the next morning with the sealed message tucked inside his robe. But Brother John stopped to collect another monk for the journey, and that monk's house was under quarantine — a suspected outbreak of plague. The city officials sealed the house with both monks inside it, following the law with the impersonal efficiency of bureaucracy, and Brother John sat in a locked room with an urgent letter in his pocket and no way to send it onward.

He returned it to Friar Lawrence the next afternoon, the seal still unbroken.

Friar Lawrence held the letter in his hands for a moment, doing the arithmetic. Juliet would wake in the tomb within hours. Romeo did not know. He reached for paper and ink — another letter, faster messenger — and simultaneously sent for a crowbar, because whether or not the message arrived in time, someone needed to be at that tomb when Juliet woke.

In Mantua, Romeo was walking in the market square when his servant Balthasar rode in with the dust of the road on him and grief on his face. Balthasar had been in Verona. He had seen the funeral procession, had seen Juliet carried to the tomb. He had not known about any plan.

"Her body sleeps in Capulet's monument," Balthasar said, still breathing hard from the ride, "and her immortal part with angels lives."

Romeo stood completely still for the space of three heartbeats. Then he said, "Is it so? Then I defy you, stars." His voice was very calm and very final. He asked Balthasar where he could buy poison in Mantua. He had made up his mind in those three seconds, and nothing in the universe was going to change it now.

8.title: Chapter 8: The Tomb
8.text:
The Capulet tomb stood at the edge of the churchyard, a heavy stone door set into a hill, older than anyone in Verona could remember. Romeo arrived at it near midnight, carrying a torch, a crowbar, and a small bottle of poison that a Mantuan apothecary had sold him with visible reluctance and audible qualifications about the speed with which it worked.

Balthasar stood at the churchyard gate, unhappy. He had been told to leave, to carry a letter to Montague, not to watch — and he had agreed to all of it, but he stayed anyway, unable to make himself walk away from whatever was about to happen.

Paris was already at the tomb when Romeo arrived. Paris had come quietly, with a pageboy and a handful of flowers — he had loved Juliet in his way, the way of a man who had planned a future with her and found it removed without his consent. He was mourning her honestly and privately, and when his pageboy whistled the alarm signal, Paris stepped from the shadows and saw the Montague boy with a crowbar at the tomb door and understood it as desecration.

"Stop thy unhallowed toil, vile Montague!" he said, stepping forward. "Can vengeance be pursued further than death? Condemned villain, I do apprehend thee."

Romeo tried to turn him away. He was past fighting, past caring about anything except getting inside that door — he told Paris to leave, told him he had come only to do what he needed to do, told him not to tempt a desperate man. Paris drew his sword.

The fight was short. Paris fell. As he died, he asked to be laid in the tomb beside Juliet — he loved her even here, at the last. Romeo, kneeling over the body of a man he had never wanted to harm, said: "I'll bury thee in a triumphant grave," and carried him inside.

The torchlight fell across the tomb's interior: cold stone, the smell of centuries, and on a raised bier at the far end, Juliet. She lay so still. She lay with her color so perfectly preserved, her lips so impossibly red, that Romeo stopped in the doorway and felt something twist in his chest — not grief, not yet, but something that almost looked like hope and was only its ghost.

He went to her. He knelt beside the bier and held her hand and looked at her face, which was as beautiful as the night in the garden. Death had not touched her. Death had not dared.

"Thou art not conquered; beauty's ensign yet is crimson in thy lips and in thy cheeks," he whispered. He was aware of the absurdity of speaking to her. He spoke anyway. "Here, here will I remain with worms that are thy chamberlains. O, here will I set up my everlasting rest and shake the yoke of inauspicious stars from this world-wearied flesh."

He raised the bottle. "O true apothecary! Thy drugs are quick." He drank.

Friar Lawrence arrived at the churchyard at a run, his robes catching on the stones, his torch throwing wild shadows across the graves. He could see from the door that something was wrong — Paris's pageboy was hiding in the churchyard, white-faced, and there was the shape of a body near the tomb entrance.

Inside, he found two bodies: Paris on the floor, Romeo on the bier beside Juliet with his hand still in hers and the empty vial beside him. The friar dropped to his knees. He pressed his hands to Romeo's chest, his throat — nothing. The poison was already done. He had been minutes too late. Minutes.

Juliet's hand moved.

She woke the way the sleeping wake — slowly, in stages, confused. She saw the friar first, his frightened face leaning over her. She asked for Romeo.

He told her, as gently as he could, that Romeo lay beside her. That plans had gone wrong. That there was no time — she could hear the watchmen coming, their torches visible through the cracks in the door, and she needed to come with him now, immediately, he would take her somewhere safe—

She was already looking at Romeo. His face was still. His hand was cooling. She could smell the poison on his lips.

"Go, get thee hence, for I will not away," she told the Friar. Her voice was perfectly steady. He hesitated, then the sound of the watchmen grew louder, and he fled.

Juliet looked at Romeo for a long moment. She tried his lips for any trace of the poison — there was none left, not enough to help her, and she almost wanted to laugh at the completeness of it. He had taken all of it. He had always been thorough.

She found his dagger. She held it for a moment, and did not think about anything very long or complicated, because the math was simple and she had always been quick at figures.

"O happy dagger," she said, "this is thy sheath."

She fell across his body as the watchmen's torches filled the tomb with light.

The Prince came. Both families came, dragged from their beds in the grey hours before dawn, still half-dressed, their faces confused with sleep and then with horror. They stood on opposite sides of their children's bodies in the cold stone tomb, and the Friar told the story from the beginning — all of it, holding nothing back, his voice flat with exhaustion and grief.

Lord Montague wept. Lord Capulet stood very still. They looked at each other across the bodies, these two old men who had spent their lives feeding a hatred neither of them could fully remember the origin of, and something in them both went out, like a candle in a closing room.

Capulet reached out his hand. "O brother Montague, give me thy hand. This is my daughter's jointure, for no more can I demand."

"But I can give thee more," said Montague, "for I will raise her statue in pure gold, that while Verona by that name is known, there shall no figure at such rate be set as that of true and faithful Juliet."

"As rich shall Romeo's by his lady's lie," Capulet said.

The Prince looked at them both with something beyond exhaustion. "A glooming peace this morning with it brings," he said. "The sun for sorrow will not show his head. Go hence to have more talk of these sad things; some shall be pardoned and some punished, for never was a story of more woe than this of Juliet and her Romeo."

The families left together, for the first time in living memory walking the same road. The tomb stood open behind them in the growing light, and the city of Verona began, slowly, to wake.

1.title: Chapter 1: A Study in Introductions
1.text:
I had returned from Afghanistan with a Jezail bullet lodged somewhere near my shoulder and a constitution considerably shaken by the rigours of the campaign. London in 1881 was a city that swallowed men whole, and I was, for a time, quite swallowed. I had rooms in a private hotel in the Strand, living poorly upon the modest pension afforded to invalided officers of Her Majesty's Army. It was a young colleague at Bart's who first uttered the name that would reshape my life entirely.

"You've been in Afghanistan, I perceive," said the lean, hawk-faced man who turned from his chemical bench as Stamford led me into the laboratory. He shook my hand with a grip that belied his wiry frame and fixed me with a pair of eyes that were at once distant and utterly penetrating.

His name was Sherlock Holmes, and he was looking for someone to share rooms with him at 221B Baker Street.

The rooms themselves were comfortable — a pair of armchairs drawn before a good fire, a deal table littered with test tubes and retorts, a violin case propped in the corner, and a Persian slipper on the mantelpiece stuffed with rough-cut tobacco. My new companion was a man of violent contrasts. For days on end he would sit motionless in his armchair, his grey eyes half-closed, his fingertips pressed together beneath his chin in that characteristic attitude of meditation. Then some case would arrive — a letter, a telegram, a knock at the door — and he would spring into action with the galvanic energy of a man possessed.

"You have been in Afghanistan, I perceive," he said again, when I pressed him on that first extraordinary greeting. "You carry yourself like a military man. Your left arm was recently injured and you favour it. Your tan ends at the wrist. The rest required no great effort."

I was astonished. He was dismissive of my astonishment. He showed me his monographs — one on the distinction between the ashes of one hundred and forty different varieties of tobacco, another on the tracing of footsteps, a third on the influence of a trade upon the form of the hand. He had catalogued the criminal history of London with the thoroughness of a naturalist cataloguing beetles.

"When I have eliminated the impossible," he said to me one evening, poking at the fire with the iron, "whatever remains, however improbable, must be the truth. That is the only principle I require." He smiled then, thin and precise. "The rest is merely observation, Watson. You see, but you do not observe."

He was infuriating. He was remarkable. Within a fortnight, I understood that I had fallen into the orbit of the most singular intellect in England, and that my quiet life of convalescence was very nearly over.

2.title: Chapter 2: A Scandal in Bohemia
2.text:
To Sherlock Holmes she is always the woman. I have seldom heard him mention her under any other name. He was not a man given to sentiment — love he regarded as a dangerous distraction, a grit in the finely calibrated mechanism of his reason. And yet there was one woman who, in his own singular phrase, eclipsed and predominated the whole of her sex.

Her name was Irene Adler, and the matter began on a March evening when a masked gentleman of unmistakably royal bearing arrived at Baker Street in a hired brougham. He was the King of Bohemia, though he maintained his disguise poorly. He had a problem of the most delicate kind: Irene Adler, a celebrated contralto of American birth, possessed a compromising photograph — the King and the woman, together — and she had threatened to send it to the princess to whom His Majesty was soon to be betrothed.

Holmes listened with his fingers steepled, his eyes half-closed. "You have been in Bohemia, I perceive," he murmured, and the King's protestations of anonymity dissolved at once.

Holmes set to work with the relish of a chess player given a complex problem. He disguised himself first as a groom, then as a clergyman, drawing me into the affair as his confederate. I was to throw a plumber's smoke rocket through Irene Adler's sitting room window at the appointed moment, then shout "Fire!" He explained the psychology with satisfaction: a woman's first instinct in a fire is to seize whatever is most precious to her.

The plan worked — partially. Holmes saw clearly where Irene Adler went to retrieve her most prized possession, behind a sliding panel in the fireplace wall. He was triumphant as we walked back through the night streets.

The next morning we arrived at her house with the King and found it empty. She had recognised Holmes, disguised as she was herself — she had passed him on the Baker Street steps the previous evening in the clothes of a young man and bade him good night. The photograph was gone. But in its place she had left one of herself alone, and a letter.

"Mr. Sherlock Holmes," it read. "You really did it very well. You took me in completely."

Holmes read the letter twice, then stood with it a moment. He asked the King only for Irene Adler's photograph as his fee — not the one showing both of them together, but a portrait of the woman alone. He kept it always.

I never heard him speak slightingly of her again. And I never heard him deny that she had beaten him.

3.title: Chapter 3: The Red-Headed League
3.text:
It was in the autumn of last year that my friend Mr. Sherlock Holmes received a visit from a stout, florid-faced, elderly gentleman with fiery red hair. His name was Jabez Wilson, and he was a pawnbroker of Coburg Square, and his story was among the strangest I have encountered in all my years of recording Holmes's cases.

Some weeks previously, Wilson told us, his assistant — a sharp young man named Vincent Spaulding who worked for him at half wages — had drawn his attention to an advertisement in the newspaper. The Red-Headed League, it announced, had a vacancy. Candidates must be adult males, sound in body and mind, and possessed of a vivid shade of red hair. The salary was four pounds per week for purely nominal work — copying out the Encyclopaedia Britannica in longhand, four hours each morning, at the League's offices in Pope's Court, Fleet Street.

Wilson had presented himself and been immediately appointed. For eight weeks he had dutifully transcribed Abbots, Archery, Architecture, Armour, and was well into the letter B when he arrived one morning to find a card pinned to the door: THE RED-HEADED LEAGUE IS DISSOLVED.

Holmes listened without moving. Then he leapt to his feet, rubbed his hands together, and declared it one of the most remarkable cases of the year.

He and I walked to Coburg Square that afternoon. Holmes spent several minutes examining the pawnbroker's shop, the surrounding streets, the buildings on either side. He tapped his cane on the pavement. He turned to me with the light of suppressed excitement in his eyes.

"Spaulding," he said quietly, as we walked away. "Tell me, Watson — what is directly behind Wilson's pawnshop?"

It was the City and Suburban Bank.

That night we were four in the bank vaults — Holmes, myself, Inspector Jones of Scotland Yard, and Mr. Merryweather, the bank director. We sat in darkness for nearly an hour before the cobblestones in the floor cracked open and a lantern flickered up from below, followed by John Clay, one of the most accomplished criminals in London, who had been digging a tunnel from Wilson's cellar to the bank for ten weeks under the cover of the Red-Headed League.

Holmes had his man on the floor and in irons within moments.

"You see, Watson," he said as we rode home through the empty streets, "it was the very brilliance of the scheme that gave it away. A genius always overreaches himself. The more elaborate the preparation, the more clearly it points to what is being protected.

4.title: Chapter 4: The Speckled Band
4.text:
Of all the problems which have been submitted to my friend Mr. Sherlock Holmes for solution during the years of our association, I cannot recall any which presented more sinister features than that which was associated with the well-known Surrey family of the Roylotts of Stoke Moran. It is true that I had seen Holmes working with cold and masterful precision on many a difficult case, but never had I seen him wear upon his face an expression of such absolute concentration as he did throughout that grim investigation.

The young woman arrived before eight in the morning, dressed entirely in black, her face white as milk, her eyes wide with that particular terror of someone who has not slept and knows why. Her name was Helen Stoner. Her twin sister Julia had died two years before under circumstances that had never been explained, and she feared she was next.

Her stepfather, Dr. Grimesby Roylott, was a man of violent passions — he had served time in prison in India for the death of his butler, and since his return to England had terrorised the neighbourhood. He kept exotic animals from his travels loose about the estate: a cheetah and a baboon that wandered the grounds freely. Julia had died in the night, screaming, crying out of a low whistle in the dark, and the words "speckled band." The coroner found nothing. No marks of violence. The doors were locked from the inside.

Now Helen Stoner was to be moved into her sister's old room, and she had begun to hear the same low whistle in the night.

Holmes's eyes were bright as we drove down to Surrey. He studied the room with the thoroughness of a man reading a text, noting the ventilator that opened not to the outside air but to the adjoining room — the stepfather's bedroom. The bell-pull that was a dummy, attached to nothing. The bed bolted to the floor directly beneath the ventilator.

We kept our vigil that night in darkness. Around three in the morning, Holmes struck a match and his cane descended again and again on something that thrashed and writhed in the ventilator shaft above us. A long, terrible scream came from the next room.

Dr. Roylott was found dead in his chair, a speckled band of yellow and brown wound twice about his brow. It was a swamp adder — the deadliest snake in India. He had trained it to descend the bell-pull and return when he whistled. That night, when Holmes struck it, it had turned upon its master.

"Violence does, in truth, recoil upon the violent," Holmes said quietly, closing the door. "And the schemer falls into the pit which he digs for another.

5.title: Chapter 5: The Five Orange Pips
5.text:
When I glance over my notes and records of the cases of Sherlock Holmes, I am faced with so many which present strange and interesting features that it is no easy matter to know which to choose and which to leave. There was, however, one case which struck Holmes himself as lying among the strangest he ever encountered, and it came to us on a wild, tempestuous night in late September.

John Openshaw arrived at Baker Street soaked to the skin, his face ashen with a terror he had been carrying for some time. His tale went back three years to his uncle Elias, a man who had lived for years in America and returned to England with considerable means and a deep, nameless dread. One morning Elias had received a letter postmarked Pondicherry. Inside were five dried orange pips and a single line: "Put the papers on the sundial." He was dead within three days — found drowned in a shallow garden pool.

John's father Joseph inherited the estate and in due course received his own letter, postmarked Dundee, with five orange pips and the same instruction. He too was dead within a fortnight, fallen from a chalk cliff.

Now John himself had received an envelope postmarked London. Five orange pips. "Put the papers on the sundial."

Holmes's face was grim as he listened. He pressed his fingertips together and was silent for a long minute. "The KKK," he said at last. "The Ku Klux Klan. Your uncle was a member in the American South. He brought papers home — perhaps records of membership, of crimes committed — and they have been hunting them down, one by one."
