He instructed John Openshaw to do exactly as the letter demanded, to leave the papers at the sundial, and to return in the morning. It was the best protection he could offer — to appear to comply while Holmes worked.

John Openshaw never returned. His body was found the next morning, drowned in the Thames, at a point close to where a three-mast barque called the Lone Star had been moored that evening.

Holmes sat in silence for a long time after Inspector Lestrade brought the news. Then he took a sheet of paper and wrote five orange pips onto it, addressed it to the captain of the Lone Star, care of a port in Savannah, Georgia.

"They will receive their answer," he said. But his voice was flat. He did not like losing. He did not like it at all.

6.title: Chapter 6: The Blue Carbuncle
6.text:
It was in the second morning after Christmas that I was drawn back to Baker Street by the sight of a good fat goose and a battered felt hat which had been left with Peterson, the commissionaire, after a scuffle in the Tottenham Court Road. Peterson had been witness to the affray and had brought Holmes both the hat and the goose — the former having belonged to a tall man of middle age, the latter being destined for a Mrs. Henry Baker.

Holmes turned the hat over in his long fingers and in three minutes had drawn from it the entire history of its owner: a man of considerable intellect who had come down in the world, with a wife who no longer loved him, who drank, but who had recently had a moral regurgitation and seen some improvement. Peterson could not find any Henry Baker in the London directory and so Holmes kept both hat and goose.

The goose was sent to the commissionaire's wife for Christmas dinner. It was she who discovered, in its crop, the blue carbuncle — one of the finest stones in existence, valued at twenty thousand pounds, recently stolen from the Countess of Morcar at the Hotel Cosmopolitan, for which a plumber named John Horner had been arrested.

Holmes's eyes lit up. He sent Peterson to replace the goose and set about tracing the bird's provenance through a chain of middlemen, each inquiry leading closer to the source. Through a salesman in Covent Garden, then a poulterer in Brixton, then a man named Ryder who came pale and trembling to Baker Street that evening and confessed all.

He had stolen the stone and hidden it in the crop of a goose belonging to his sister — the wrong goose had been taken from the flock and sold. Holmes listened to his confession with no particular expression on his face.

"Get out," Holmes said quietly, when Ryder had finished. The man stared. "I will not appear against you. You will leave London tonight and not return. John Horner will have to be released for lack of evidence." He studied the carbuncle in the lamplight. "This is the season of forgiveness. I am not retained by the police to supply their deficiencies."

After Ryder had fled, I asked Holmes if he feared he was helping a guilty man escape justice.

He smiled and set the stone down. "Perhaps. And yet consider — what is the use of ruining a frightened young man's life over one crime of desperation? Chance has put in our way a most singular and whimsical problem. We have had our adventure for the afternoon. I think that for tonight I shall be satisfied with that.

7.title: Chapter 7: The Copper Beeches
7.text:
One morning Miss Violet Hunter, a young woman of a most engaging and prepossessing appearance, called upon Holmes to ask his advice on whether she should accept a position being offered to her — a post as governess to a young child at the Copper Beeches, a house five miles west of Winchester, at a salary of one hundred pounds per year, which was nearly double what she might expect. The employer, one Mr. Jephro Rucastle, was a cheerful, florid man who had nonetheless attached two singular conditions to her employment: she must cut her hair short, and she must be prepared to wear a particular electric-blue dress whenever he required it.

Holmes was quiet for a moment after she finished. "I confess that I am not entirely easy in my mind about this," he told her. "These are odd conditions. But if you are in want of money, and if the house is otherwise satisfactory, I would not dissuade you outright. Send me a telegram if anything strikes you as wrong."

Miss Hunter accepted the post and the telegrams began arriving within a fortnight.

She had cut her hair. She wore the dress when directed and sat facing the window while Mr. Rucastle stood behind her, laughing enormously at something in the road. She had glimpsed, in a mirror, a young man outside who watched the house. There was a large wing locked and shuttered at the far end of the house. There was a mastiff chained in the yard at night. And she had found, hidden in a locked drawer, a coil of rich auburn hair — a perfect match for her own, before it was cut.

Holmes and I were on the Winchester train within the hour.

The mystery resolved itself with the methodical clarity Holmes always brought to tangled affairs. The locked wing concealed Alice Rucastle, Mr. Rucastle's daughter by his first marriage, who was being held prisoner to prevent her from marrying a young man she loved and thus controlling her own inheritance. Miss Hunter had been engaged to impersonate Alice — the same hair, the same blue dress — to convince the young man watching from the road that his Alice was well and happy and had forgotten him.

We freed Alice Rucastle that night, the mastiff dispatched by its own keeper when it turned on its master. Holmes said very little on the return journey, staring out at the dark Hampshire countryside with a certain remote dissatisfaction, as he sometimes did when a case resolved into mere domestic wickedness rather than the grander criminal architecture he preferred.

"Crime is common," he said at last, half to himself. "Logic is rare. It is upon logic that we must dwell, rather than upon the crimes themselves.

8.title: Chapter 8: The Final Reflection
8.text:
I have been asked, more than once, what it is like to live alongside the foremost criminal investigator of the age, and I confess the question still gives me pause. The popular image — the hawk-nosed figure in the deerstalker, the magnifying glass, the thundering declarations of the obvious — is not wrong, exactly, but it is incomplete in the way all portraits are incomplete.

Holmes was a man of violent contrasts, as I have said. He could go three days without food when a case consumed him entirely, then eat enormously and without decorum at its conclusion. He could sit still as a carved idol for an hour while working through a problem in his mind, then explode from his chair and be in a hansom cab before I had found my hat. He played the violin beautifully, and at three in the morning, when he could not sleep — which was often — he would play long, melancholic improvisations that drifted down the stair like smoke.

He scorned sentiment as a weakness in any professional, but he was not, I think, without feeling. I saw him stand very still over the body of a young woman once, who had been brought to him too late, and his face in that moment was not the face of a machine. He never spoke of it.

What he possessed above all other gifts — above his knowledge of chemistry and criminal history, above his capacity for disguise, above even that extraordinary analytical intelligence — was the ability to see. Not merely to look, as he was fond of pointing out to me, but truly to observe. To gather the ten thousand small facts that the world presents each moment and find, among them, the one constellation that tells the true story.

"You see, Watson," he said to me once, near the end of a long evening when a case had finally resolved and we sat by the fire with our pipes, "the world is full of obvious things which nobody by any chance ever observes. I have trained myself to observe them. That is all."

It was not all, of course. But I had long since stopped arguing with him on such points.

What I can say, having recorded these adventures as faithfully as I am able, is that the years at Baker Street were unlike any other years of my life. There were nights of great danger and mornings of grey tedium. There were cases that turned on a single strand of mud from a peculiar district, and cases that turned on the entire history of a continent. There was never a dull week.

I think sometimes of that first morning in the laboratory at Bart's, when a strange young man turned from his chemical bench and said, without introduction or ceremony: "You've been in Afghanistan, I perceive." I did not know then what that sentence would cost me, or what it would give me. I know now.

Whatever else may be said of Sherlock Holmes — and a great deal has been said, and more will be — he was, in his own peculiar and irreplaceable way, a great man. And I was fortunate, all my life, to have been his friend.
